sections.section_title: Subgroup analyses of beverage consumption and dementia risk
sections.section_text: 3.5 Subgroup analyses of beverage consumption and dementia risk Fig. 2 presents forest plots from subgroup analyses examining the associations between beverage consumption intake and the risk of ACD, stratified by key modifiable risk factors, including hypertension, obesity, depression, and dyslipidemia. Fig. 2 Forest plots showing interactions between significant modifiable risk factors and beverage intake for all-cause dementia. A: Sugar-sweetened beverages (SSBs), B: Artificially sweetened beverages (ASBs), C: Natural juices (NJs), D: Coffee, E: Tea. Obesity was defined as BMI ≥ 30 kg/m 2 . Subgroup analyses were adjusted for age, sex, ethnicity, Townsend deprivation index, household income, education level, smoking status, alcohol intake, physical activity, body mass index, glucose, total cholesterol, low-density lipoprotein cholesterol, high-density lipoprotein cholesterol, triglyceride, comorbidities (hypertension, diabetes, and cardiovascular disease), and APOE ε4 carrier status. HR, hazard ratio; CI, confidence interval; APOE, apolipoprotein E. Fig. 2 A significant interaction was observed between depression and SSB intake ( P -interaction = 0.030). Among participants with depression, even moderate SSB intake (>0–1 glass/day) was associated with a 76% increased risk of ACD (HR, 1.76; 95% CI, 1.11–2.79). Higher SSB intake (>1 glass/day) was also associated with an elevated risk of ACD in this group (HR, 1.46; 95% CI, 0.69–3.10), although the association was not statistically significant. Among individuals without depression, high SSB consumption (>1 glass/day) was associated with a 62% increased risk of ACD (HR, 1.62; 95% CI, 1.28–2.06). A significant interaction was also found between dyslipidemia and ASBs ( P -interaction = 0.047). Among individuals with dyslipidemia, ASB intake was associated with an increased risk of ACD, with HRs of 1.50 (95% CI, 1.12–2.03) for >0–1 glass/day and 1.80 (95% CI, 1.13–2.85) for >1 glass/day. Coffee consumption was significantly associated with a reduced risk of ACD, particularly among individuals with hypertension and obesity. The protective effect was more pronounced in those with obesity: HRs were 0.38 (95% CI, 0.23–0.61) for >0–1 glass/day and 0.51 (95% CI, 0.34–0.77) for >1 glass/day ( P -interaction = 0.009). In participants with hypertension, the corresponding HRs were 0.53 (95% CI, 0.42–0.67) and 0.69 (95% CI, 0.55–0.86) ( P -interaction = 0.012). Similarly, tea consumption showed a protective association in participants with hypertension and obesity. Among those with hypertension, tea intake was associated with a reduced risk of ACD: HRs were 0.67 (95% CI, 0.51–0.87) for >0–1 glass/day and 0.66 (95% CI, 0.54–0.79) for >1 glass/day ( P -interaction = 0.031). In individuals with obesity, the protective association was also significant: HRs were 0.56 (95% CI, 0.33–0.93) for >0–1 glass/day and 0.48 (95% CI, 0.33–0.69) for >1 glass/day ( P -interaction = 0.020). Supplementary Fig. S4 displays subgroup analyses stratified by diabetes and chronic kidney disease, showing no significant interactions between these conditions and beverage consumption in relation to ACD risk. Supplementary Tables S7 and S8 present subgroup analyses for the associations between beverage intake and the risks of AD and VaD, respectively, stratified by modifiable risk factors.

5 Conclusion In this large prospective cohort study, replacing SSBs or ASBs with coffee or tea was associated with a lower risk of dementia, particularly among individuals with modifiable risk factors such as obesity, hypertension, depression, and dyslipidemia. These findings highlight the potential of targeted beverage substitution as a simple and practical strategy for mitigating the risk of dementia. Given the global rise in prevalence of dementia and the modifiable nature of beverage habits, this strategy may have meaningful public health implications. Further research is warranted to confirm these associations and elucidate the underlying mechanisms.

Introduction Oxidative stress occurs when there is an imbalance between reactive oxygen species (ROS) and antioxidants in the body, leading to cellular damage [ 1 ]. This imbalance is associated with aging and the development of chronic diseases, including cancer, cardiovascular diseases, and neurodegenerative conditions [ 2 – 4 ]. Tea, particularly green tea, is rich in antioxidants, which have been shown to neutralize free radicals and reduce oxidative stress [ 5 ]. These antioxidants include polyphenols like catechins in green tea and theaflavins in black tea. These compounds have been found to help in the prevention of oxidative damage to cells and tissues, contributing to improved health outcomes. This review aims to summarize the research progress on the regulation of oxidative stress by different molecular mechanisms between black tea and green tea. Key areas include direct scavenging of ROS, activation of endogenous antioxidant defence, inhibition of pro-oxidant enzymes and inflammatory signaling pathways, sequestration of metal ions and protection of mitochondrial function. Unlike earlier reviews, this paper provides a side-by-side comparison of the composition and molecular mechanisms of green and black tea. Evidence from in vitro studies, in vivo models, and clinical trials is discussed, highlighting their differential biological activities and potential for tailored therapeutic applications.

Background Biological basis of oxidative stress The concept of oxidative stress was developed in 1985 as a concept in redox biology and medicine [ 6 ]. Originally defined as a disturbance in the prooxidant-antioxidant balance in favor of the former. Advances in experimental methods have refined oxidative-stress theory to the molecular level, and we now consider oxidative stress to be an imbalance between the production of ROS and the body’s ability to neutralize or repair the damage caused by ROS [ 1 ]. ROS are by-products of cellular metabolism, including free radicals such as superoxide anion (O₂•-), hydroxyl radicals (OH•), and non-radical substances like hydrogen peroxide. At physiological levels, these substances serve signalling and host-defence functions; however, when produced in excess, they overwhelms antioxidant defenses and causes oxidative modification of membranes, lipids, proteins, lipoproteins and DNA, contributing to diverse pathologies [ 7 ]. Furthermore, oxidative stress plays a key role in cancer by inducing DNA mutations, promoting tumourigenesis and influencing metastatic processes [ 8 ]. Antioxidant compounds in tea Tea contains a large number of polyphenolic compounds, and these natural antioxidants have received widespread attention because of their unique molecular structures and biological activities.Green tea is mainly rich in catechin compounds, such as epigallocatechin-3-gallate (EGCG) and epicatechin (ECG), which have extremely strong free radical scavenging and anti-inflammatory effects; whereas black tea has been fermented to produce compounds with high stability and dual antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties [ 5 , 9 ]. Due to these differences, black tea and green tea exhibit different action characteristics in regulating oxidative stress [ 10 ]. Tea also contains other polyphenols, such as gallic acid, chlorogenic acid, ellagic acid, alloy quinic acid, kaempferol-3-O-glucoside (kaempferol-3-G), and various flavonoids [ 11 ]. And these diverse polyphenols collectively broaden its antioxidant and anti-inflammatory repertoire and support its potential to protect cellular health and mitigate oxidative damage. Besides polyphenols, black and green teas contain amino acids, alkaloids, polysaccharides and trace elements. These components also assist tea in its antioxidant defence role [ 12 ].L-Theanine is a special amino acid found in tea, which is similar in chemical structure to the brain-active substances glutamine and glutamic acid [ 13 ].L-Theanine have significant biological activity and can regulate a variety of physiological function. Particularly in neuroprotection, L-Theanine have potential protective effects against damage associated with Parkinson’s disease (PD) and Alzheimer’s disease (AD).The main alkaloid in tea is caffeine, which has multiple effects on physiological processes, including the circulatory, respiratory, digestive, endocrine, metabolic and central nervous systems [ 14 ].

Antioxidant compounds in tea Tea contains a large number of polyphenolic compounds, and these natural antioxidants have received widespread attention because of their unique molecular structures and biological activities.Green tea is mainly rich in catechin compounds, such as epigallocatechin-3-gallate (EGCG) and epicatechin (ECG), which have extremely strong free radical scavenging and anti-inflammatory effects; whereas black tea has been fermented to produce compounds with high stability and dual antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties [ 5 , 9 ]. Due to these differences, black tea and green tea exhibit different action characteristics in regulating oxidative stress [ 10 ]. Tea also contains other polyphenols, such as gallic acid, chlorogenic acid, ellagic acid, alloy quinic acid, kaempferol-3-O-glucoside (kaempferol-3-G), and various flavonoids [ 11 ]. And these diverse polyphenols collectively broaden its antioxidant and anti-inflammatory repertoire and support its potential to protect cellular health and mitigate oxidative damage. Besides polyphenols, black and green teas contain amino acids, alkaloids, polysaccharides and trace elements. These components also assist tea in its antioxidant defence role [ 12 ].L-Theanine is a special amino acid found in tea, which is similar in chemical structure to the brain-active substances glutamine and glutamic acid [ 13 ].L-Theanine have significant biological activity and can regulate a variety of physiological function. Particularly in neuroprotection, L-Theanine have potential protective effects against damage associated with Parkinson’s disease (PD) and Alzheimer’s disease (AD).The main alkaloid in tea is caffeine, which has multiple effects on physiological processes, including the circulatory, respiratory, digestive, endocrine, metabolic and central nervous systems [ 14 ].

Direct scavenging of ROS Tea polyphenols neutralize ROS through direct chemical reactions enabled by their unique structures. In essence, the multiple hydroxyl groups present on molecules like EGCG (epigallocatechin-3-gallate) allow these compounds to donate hydrogen atoms or electrons to free radicals. This donation transforms highly reactive ROS, such as hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide anions (O₂•⁻), into more stable, to less harmful phenoxyl radicals. The effectiveness of this mechanism has been demonstrated by electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy [ 15 ]. And it shows that the oxidation of the D-ring in EGCG’s galloyl group is key to its radical-scavenging ability [ 16 ]. This technique also demonstrated that EGCG and ECG containing gallic acid moieties had the highest activity to scavenge ROS, while EC had lower activity [ 16 ]. These direct ROS-scavenging actions support tea polyphenols’ protective effects against oxidative stress and related cellular damage. In vitro chemical methods (e.g., DPPH, FRAP, and ORAC) are commonly used to quantify the direct antioxidant capacity of tea polyphenols [ 17 ]. And they confirmed that the antioxidant capacity of tea is directly related to its polyphenol content [ 18 – 21 ]. DPPH reflects the rate and extent of electron/hydrogen supply to free radicals; FRAP determines the reducing power of samples under acidic conditions; and ORAC evaluates the termination and persistence of the chain reaction to peroxyl radicals with fluorescence-kinetics curves [ 22 ]. The DPPH and FRAP methods are easy to operate and the ORAC is more compatible with the physiological environment. As each assay detects different mechanisms, it is recommended that at least two complementary methods be used for a comprehensive assessment [ 23 ]. But recent research suggests that tea polyphenols regulate ROS by a multiple signaling pathway modulation mechanism, rather than relying solely on the direct removal of it.

sections.section_title: Activation of endogenous antioxidant defence system
sections.section_text: Activation of endogenous antioxidant defence system Tea polyphenols modulate the endogenous antioxidant system mainly dependent on the nuclear factor Nrf2/ARE signaling pathway and the glutathione system (Figs. 1 and 2 ). Fig. 1 Schematic illustration of the mechanisms related to the regulation of PI3K/Akt, Nrf-2/Keap1, cAMP/PKA and NF-κB cell signaling pathways by green tea polyphenols (GTP). And the antioxidant enzymes mentioned in the figure include heme oxygenase-1(OH-1), NAD (P)H:quinone oxidoreductase 1(NQO1), glutathione(GSH), superoxide dismutase(SOD). BDNF, brain-derived neurotrophic factor; CREB, cAMP-response element binding protein; ARE, antioxidant response elements; Nrf2, nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2; Keap1, kelch-like ECH-associated protein 1; cAMP, cyclic adenosine 3’,5’-monophosphate ; PKA, protein kinase A; PI3K, phosphoinositide 3-kinase; Akt(PKB), protein kinase B; MST1/2, macrophage stimulating 1/2; SIRT1, NAD-dependent deacetylase sirtuin-1; FOXO3, forkhead box O3; NF-κB, nuclear factor kappa-B. Studies have shown that under normal conditions, Nrf2 binds to its inhibitory protein Keap1 and keeps activity low. When cells are subjected to oxidative stress, tea polyphenols are able to disrupt the interaction between Nrf2 and Keap1, stabilize Nrf2 and migrate it to the nucleus. In nucleus, it binds to ARE sequences to induce a series of antioxidative activities, including HMOX1, SOD and CAT, thereby neutralizing the overproduction of ROS [ 24 – 27 ]. Meanwhile, tea polyphenols are able to modulate upstream regulators, such as through activation of signaling pathways such as ERK1/2, further promoting Nrf2 activation and intranuclear localization [ 24 , 28 , 29 ]. Recent studies indicate that tea polyphenols also interact with additional signaling pathways that fine-tune the antioxidant response, attenuating pro-oxidant enzyme expression while enhancing protective protein synthesis [ 30 , 31 ]. This multi-targeted approach not only reduces cellular damage and inflammation but also promotes cell survival under stress conditions. In addition to Nrf2/ARE signaling pathway, tea polyphenols further maintain cellular redox status by enhancing glutathione peroxidase (GSH-Px) activity and regulating GSH/GSSG balance [ 32 ]. Li et al. (2014) found that in a methylmercury-induced neurotoxicity model, tea polyphenol pretreatment restored GSH-Px and GR activity in astrocytes while reducing ROS production, suggesting that it attenuates oxidative damage by maintaining GSH cycling [ 33 ]. In addition, tea polyphenols alleviated lead exposure-induced oxidative damage in the kidney by inhibiting the ROS-mediated JNK-MAPK pathway, reducing lipid peroxides malondialdehyde (MDA) levels, and restoring GSH levels [ 29 , 34 ]. Thus, tea polyphenols constitute a multifaceted antioxidant defence network by relying on the regulation of the Nrf2/ARE signaling pathway and the glutathione system, And it provides an important molecular target for the development of natural antioxidants and anti-inflammatory drugs.

sections.section_title: Inhibition of pro-oxidant enzymes and inflammatory signaling pathways
sections.section_text: Inhibition of pro-oxidant enzymes and inflammatory signaling pathways In a state of oxidative stress, activation of pro-oxidant enzymes and inflammatory signaling pathways further exacerbates ROS generation and inflammatory responses. Black tea polyphenols have been shown to inhibit TLR4/NF-κB p65 pathway, thereby reducing inflammation and apoptosis [ 35 ](Fig. 2 ). In addition, tea polyphenols mitigate ethionamide-induced apoptosis by inhibiting the expression of CYP450s and blocking ROS accumulation and mitochondrial damage [ 36 ]. Fig. 2 Schematic illustration of the mechanisms related to the regulation of PI3K/Akt, Nrf-2/Gpx4, TLR4/NF-κB p65,JNK and p38 MAPK cell signaling pathways by black tea polyphenols (BTP) ARE, antioxidant response elements; Nrf2, nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2; Keap1, kelch-like ECH-associated protein 1; Gpx4, glutathione peroxidase 4; NF-κB, nuclear factor kappa-B; PI3K, phosphoinositide 3-kinase; Akt(PKB), protein kinase B; JNK, c-Jun N-terminal kinase; MAPK, mitogen-activated protein kinase; FOXO3, forkhead box O3. In inflammatory regulation, tea polyphenols plays a key role by inhibiting the NF-κB pathway. Li et al. (2014) found that tea polyphenols preconditioning inhibited TLR4/NF-κB p65 signaling, reduced the levels of pro-inflammatory factors such as IL-1β and TNF-α, and attenuated renal ischaemia-reperfusion injury [ 37 ]. And Yang et al. (2024) further revealed that green tea polyphenols (GTP) reduced gastric mucosal inflammatory infiltration by inhibiting the ROS-mediated PERK/IRE-1/ATF6 endoplasmic reticulum stress pathway and blocking NF-κB activation [ 38 ]. Meanwhile, the MAPK signaling pathway (including JNK, p38 and ERK) also plays an important role in the regulation of apoptosis and stress response, and tea polyphenols can regulate the phosphorylation level of this pathway, thus inhibiting oxidative stress-induced cell apoptosis [ 39 ]. Also tea polyphenols reduces lead exposure-induced expression of inflammatory factors TNF-α and IL-6 by inhibiting the ROS/JNK-MAPK pathway [ 29 ]. Additionally, tea polyphenols have been shown to reduce neuroinflammation and oxidative damage by inhibiting the ROS-NO pathway and decreasing the expression of inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS) in a Parkinson’s disease model [ 40 , 41 ]. The signaling pathways described above form a complex interactive network rather than acting independently. Activation of MAPK and PI3K/Akt cascades can enhance Nrf2 translocation and transcriptional activity, while Nrf2-driven antioxidant responses suppress NF-κB-mediated inflammation [ 42 , 43 ]. FOXO3, modulated by Akt, regulates antioxidant enzyme expression such as SOD and CAT, linking metabolic and redox control [ 44 ]. This interconnected antioxidant defense and inflammation suppression, providing a systemic explanation for the multifaceted protective effects of tea polyphenols against oxidative stress. Tea polyphenols form a multi-level antioxidant and anti-inflammatory network by inhibiting pro-oxidant enzymes and blocking inflammatory signaling pathways such as NF-κB, providing a theoretical basis for the prevention and treatment of oxidative stress-related diseases, such as obesity, neurodegenerative diseases and cancer [ 45 – 47 ].However, its specific molecular mechanism still needs to be verified by more rigorously designed clinical studies.

Chelation of metal ions Transition metal ions, including Fe²⁺, Cu⁺, catalyze the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) via the Fenton reaction, a key driver of oxidative stress. Free Cu²⁺ can react with hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) to form highly toxic hydroxyl radicals (-OH), leading to oxidative damage to lipids, proteins and DNA [ 48 , 49 ]. In addition, metal ions can exacerbate oxidative stress in neurodegenerative diseases by binding to β-amyloid (Aβ) or α-synaptic nucleoprotein [ 50 , 51 ].Therefore, chelation of free metal ions becomes an important strategy to block oxidative damage [ 52 ]. The phenolic hydroxyl and catechol structures of tea polyphenols confer a strong metal binding capacity. The o-benzenetriol hydroxyl group on the B ring can simultaneously bind metal ions to form a stable five- or six-membered cyclic chelates [ 53 ]. Thus, tea polyphenols form stable complexes with metal ions and block their ability to participate in the Fenton reaction, reducing the generation of hydroxyl radicals [ 31 ]. Spectrophotometric studies have shown that both green and black tea extracts, at concentrations of 10–60 µg/mL, can sequester ferric ions effectively, with individual catechins displaying varying chelating strengths (EGC >ECG = EGCG >EC) [ 54 – 57 ]. Additionally, the gallate group in theaflavins plays a key role in iron binding, while copper tends to interact with the hydroxyl groups on the polyphenol rings [ 58 ]. Notably, black tea exhibits higher metal-chelating abilities compared to green tea. These chelation properties substantially contribute to the overall antioxidant potential of tea polyphenols by inhibiting metal-catalyzed ROS generation and reducing cellular oxidative damage. In addition to copper and iron ions, EGCG chelates Pb²⁺and Hg²⁺and attenuates their induced mitochondrial ROS burst and DNA damage [ 59 ]. Generally, Tea polyphenols reduce ROS generation at source by chelating Fe²⁺, Cu²⁺, Hg²⁺, Pb²⁺ and other transition metal ions and blocking their participation in redox cycles. These mechanisms provide a molecular basis for the protective role of Tea polyphenols in metabolic diseases, neurodegenerative diseases and environmental pollution toxicity.

sections.section_title: Protection of mitochondrial function and Inhibition of apoptosis
sections.section_text: Protection of mitochondrial function and Inhibition of apoptosis Mitochondria are the center of cellular energy metabolism and are also important for ROS generation. Oxidative stress leads to loss of mitochondrial membrane potential, decreased ATP production and activation of apoptotic pathways. And tea polyphenols regulate oxidative stress by protecting mitochondrial function through multiple pathways, with mechanisms involving maintenance of mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP), inhibition of mitochondria-dependent apoptotic pathways and activation of antioxidant signaling pathways. Studies have shown that tea polyphenols are effective in mitigating mitochondrial damage induced by toxins such as acetamiprid and trimethyltin chloride, expressed as inhibition of MMP decline and ATP depletion while reducing cytochrome c release from mitochondria to cytoplasm, thereby blocking caspase-3 activation [ 34 , 36 ]. In a neuronal model, tea polyphenols enhance mitochondrial function, reduce reactive oxygen species (ROS) accumulation, and inhibit oxidative stress-induced apoptosis through activation of the TrkB/CREB/BDNF signaling pathway [ 60 ]. Additionally, EGCG enhances mitochondrial antioxidant defences and attenuates radiation- or β-amyloid-induced mitochondrial dysfunction by up-regulating the expression of Nrf2 and its downstream heme oxygenase-1 (HO-1) [ 61 , 62 ]. Regulation of mitochondrial biosynthesis is also one of the key mechanisms and Valenti et al. (2013) demonstrated that EGCG promotes mitochondrial biogenesis and restores the efficiency of ATP synthesis in Down syndrome cells by activating the Sirt1/PGC-1α pathway [ 63 ]. Furthermore, tea polyphenols directly target the mitochondrial microenvironment. EGCG can accumulates and scavenges ROS in mitochondria, and Lopez et al. (2016) noting that it reduces iron overload and inhibits ROS production via the mitochondrial transferrin [ 64 , 65 ]. Meanwhile, EGCG inhibits mitochondrial ROS-triggered NLRP3 inflammasome activation and reduces oxidized mtDNA release, thereby attenuating tissue damage [ 66 ]. These multiple protection maintain mitochondrial homeostasis and ultimately alleviate oxidative stress-related neurodegenerative diseases, cardiovascular diseases and metabolic disorders [ 67 , 68 ].

sections.section_title: Regulation of autophagy and metabolic reprogramming
sections.section_text: Regulation of autophagy and metabolic reprogramming Tea polyphenols significantly alleviate oxidative stress by regulating autophagy and metabolic reprogramming through a mechanism that involves synergistic effects of multiple pathways.Studies have shown that EGCG attenuate oxidative damage by enhancing the expression of autophagy-related proteins and promoting cellular clearance of damaged components. Green tea polyphenols induce protective autophagy and reduce the accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reactive nitrogen species (RNS) in UV-induced photoaging by activating Beclin-1 and LC3-II [ 47 ]. Similarly, EGCG inhibits autophagy dysregulation and reduces the level of oxidative stress in reproductive system cancer cells by modulating autophagy-related signals such as the PI3K-AKT-mTOR pathway [ 69 ]. And some researchers further demonstrated that tea polyphenols protect cells from oxidative damage by inhibiting TOCP-induced autophagic hyperactivation in ovarian granulosa cells and decreasing ROS generation [ 70 ]. A study by Lv H et al. (2024) showed that green tea polyphenols reversed the suppression of autophagy in lung tissues due to TBBPA exposure by restoring the expression of autophagy markers, such as Beclin-1 and ATG5, as well as decreasing inflammatory factors, such as IL-1β, TNF-α, and oxidative stress markers, highlighting the key role of autophagy in antioxidant [ 71 ]. In terms of metabolic reprogramming, tea polyphenols reshape redox balance by inhibiting the Warburg effect (over-activation of glycolysis) and modulating energy metabolic pathways. It has been found that tea polyphenols alleviate metabolic imbalance-associated oxidative stress by inhibiting the CYP450s/ROS/MAPK/NF-κB pathway and reducing ethionamide-induced activity of key enzymes of glycolysis and accumulation of lactic acid [ 72 ]. EGCG induces autophagy by targeting the PI3K-AKT-mTOR pathway, and also inhibits H2O2-induced activation of glycolysis in endothelial cells, maintaining mitochondrial function and reduces ROS production [ 73 ].These studies suggest that tea polyphenols not only scavenge oxidative damage products through autophagy, but also reduce the source of ROS generation by inhibiting aberrant metabolic pathways. Generally, tea polyphenols synergistically regulate oxidative stress by activating protective autophagy and inhibiting harmful metabolic reprogramming. This multi-target mode of action provides a theoretical basis for the clinical application of tea polyphenols in antioxidant-related diseases.

sections.section_title: Differences in composition and action characteristics
sections.section_text: Differences in composition and action characteristics The compositional differences between black tea and green tea are mainly due to the differences in processing, in which fermentation significantly alters the chemical structure and biological activity of polyphenols [ 12 ]. In terms of polyphenol composition, green tea adopts a non-fermentation process, which maximizes the retention of natural polyphenol components in the fresh leaves, especially catechins, including epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG), epicatechin (EC), epigallocatechin gallate (ECG), etc [ 74 , 75 ]. In black tea, through the complete fermentation process, catechins are oxidized by enzymatic oxidation to form theaflavins and thearubigins, of which theaflavin-3,3’-digallate (TF3) is a characteristic component of black tea [ 76 ]. And its antioxidant activity is slightly lower than that of EGCG, but it has stronger anti-inflammatory characteristics [ 77 , 78 ]. In terms of caffeine content, the difference between green tea (39.764 ± 0.382 mg/DW) and black tea (35.283 ± 0.340 mg/DW) is small, but green tea has a higher content of L-theanine, an amino acid that synergizes with polyphenols to enhance neuroprotection [ 10 , 79 ]. Black tea, on the other hand, gets a unique flavour and another set of regulating mechanisms by virtue of the complex polyphenol system generated during the fermentation process [ 80 ]. In terms of signaling regulation, green tea polyphenols, especially EGCG, potently activate the Nrf2/ARE axis to modulate HO-1, SOD, CAT and GSH-related enzymes, whereas theaflavins chondrocytes alleviate iron metamorphosis through Nrf2-Gpx4 signaling. In contrast, theaflavins and thearubigins in black tea mainly inhibit inflammatory signaling to modulate oxidative stress. Black tea tea polyphenols significantly inhibited the activation of the NF-κB signaling pathway, which in turn reduced the expression of pro-inflammatory factors such as TNF-α and IL-6, thereby effectively alleviating chronic inflammatory responses triggered by long-term oxidative stress. Thus, black tea showed strong protective effects in certain models of chronic inflammation and ischaemia-reperfusion injury, whereas green tea was superior in direct scavenging of ROS and activation of endogenous antioxidant defence systems [ 10 ]. Several studies have implied that theaflavins in black tea share similar antioxidant mechanisms with green tea polyphenols. It has been shown that theaflavins in black tea mitigate iron metamorphosis by reducing inducers, up-regulating the iron storage protein FTH1, and promoting the safe chelation of iron ion, thereby decreasing intracellular levels of Fe ²⁺ and preventing iron-dependent cell death [ 81 , 82 ]. In addition to this, epigenetic regulation is another key mechanism of action. Theaflavins directly interact with DNA methyltransferases (DNMT1 and DNMT3a), which reduces overall DNA methylation [ 83 , 84 ]. These epigenetic events reactivates silenced antioxidant genes, and enhances the expression of enzymes such as SOD and GPx, which ultimately reduces ROS accumulation [ 84 , 85 ]. However, compare to green tea, research on black tea and its bioactive components is still limited and its translational potential needs further study. Together, black tea showed strong protective effects in certain models of chronic inflammation and ischaemia-reperfusion injury, whereas green tea was superior in direct scavenging of ROS and activation of endogenous antioxidant defence systems. These differences indicate potential for context-specific applications, though further work, particularly in well-designed clinical studies, is needed to clarify translational relevance (Table 1 ). Table 1 Comparison of antioxidant differences between black tea and green tea Green tea Black tea Main ingredient EGCG、ECG、EGC、EC TF、TF3G、TFDG Representative targets Nrf2/ARE、AMPK、FOXO3 NF-κB、MAPK、Nrf2/GPX4 Mechanisms of antioxidant stress Direct scavenging of ROS; activation of antioxidant enzymes; promotion of mitochondrial homeostasis Inhibits inflammatory signals; prevents lipid peroxidation and ferroptosis; protects cellular structure Downstream biological effects Improve SOD, CAT, GSH-Px; maintains ATP production and mitochondrial membrane potential Inhibit TNF-α、IL-6;Improve GPX4 and FTH1; Inhibition of apoptosis and inflammatory response Physiological characteristic Direct antioxidant, anti-aging, metabolism improvement Anti-inflammatory, anti-ferroptosis, protects cardiovascular and liver functions EGCG, Epigallocatechin gallate; ECG, Epicatechin gallate; EGC, Epigallocatechin; EC, Epicatechin; TF, Theaflavin; TF3G, Theaflavin-3-gallate; TFDG, Theaflavin-3,3’-digallate

Synergy effect Tea polyphenols, as the core active ingredients in tea, show synergistic mechanisms in synergising with other natural antioxidants [ 86 ]. It was found that green tea polyphenols significantly inhibited the generation of hydrogen peroxide-induced reactive oxygen species (ROS) and lipid peroxidation products when combined with caffeine and its antioxidant effect was superior to that of a single ingredient, suggesting that the synergistic effect of the two may originate from caffeine’s modulation of cellular membrane permeability and the polyphenol’s ability to directly scavenge free radicals [ 87 ]. One study found a dual role for melatonin in the system of action of tea polyphenols. At low concentrations, the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in melatonin and the addition of DNA inhibited this pro-oxidant effect, while at high copper concentrations, melatonin was able to reduce the generation of hydroxyl radicals and protect DNA from damage [ 88 ]. However, the mechanisms of synergy still need to be further explored. The limited bioavailability of tea polyphenols may affect their synergistic effects with other antioxidants [ 89 ].

Experimental models and clinical evidence Aiming at the role of tea polyphenols in regulating oxidative stress, a large number of in vitro, animal and clinical studies have been conducted in recent years, which have provided a rich experimental basis for revealing its mechanism of action. Green tea At the cellular level, studies using Human umbilical vein endothelial cells(HUVEC), Human SH-SY5Y neuroblastoma cells, bovine mammary epithelial cells(BMECs) and other cellular models showed that green tea polyphenol treatment significantly reduced intracellular ROS levels and up-regulated the activities of antioxidant enzymes, like SOD, CAT, and GSH-Px [ 24 , 90 – 92 ] (Table 2 ). For example, EGCG from green tea significantly improved cell survival by activating the Nrf2 pathway and down-regulating pro-inflammatory factor expression in a cell model of H₂O₂-induced oxidative damage [ 60 ]. Furthermore, in vitro assays using breast cancer, kidney cells and endothelial cells have demonstrated the positive effects of tea polyphenols in inhibiting apoptosis and improving cellular energy metabolism [ 29 , 93 – 95 ]. Animal experiments further validated the role of tea polyphenols in modulating oxidative stress in vivo. Animal models commonly used in research include ischemia-reperfusion injury model, obesity model, neurotoxicity model and chemically induced tumour model. Molina et al. (2015) demonstrated in a high-fat diet-induced obese rat model that tea polyphenols significantly elevated SOD and GSH-Px activities and reduced malondialdehyde (MDA) levels through activation of the Nrf2 pathway [ 96 ]. In a Parkinson’s disease model, Mandel et al. (2004) found that EGCG reduced midbrain dopaminergic neuronal damage by inhibiting the production of ROS and NO, and decreased the expression of the lipid peroxidation marker 3-nitrotyrosine [ 41 , 97 ]. In liver injury research, fatty liver ischemia/reperfusion (I/R) model with methylmercury-induced oxidative damage to hepatocytes has been applied to study the mechanism [ 98 , 99 ]. Black tea At the cellular level, studies using rat intestinal epithelial cells (RIE), osteoarthritic chondrocytes, human malignant melanoma cells (A375), HepG2 cells and other in vitro models have demonstrated that black tea polyphenol treatment significantly reduces the intracellular level of ROS, and up-regulates Gpx4 to enhance antioxidant activity, while inhibiting the activation of NF-κB, MAPK, and other pro-inflammatory signaling pathways [ 24 , 90 – 92 ].The animal models studied mainly focus on tumour models. The anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities of black tea have been demonstrated through various experimental models (Table 3 ). Clinical trials and limitations Clinical evidence and epidemiological studies support the potential health benefits of tea polyphenols, but also reveal challenges in their application (Table 4 ). Green tea At the cellular level, studies using Human umbilical vein endothelial cells(HUVEC), Human SH-SY5Y neuroblastoma cells, bovine mammary epithelial cells(BMECs) and other cellular models showed that green tea polyphenol treatment significantly reduced intracellular ROS levels and up-regulated the activities of antioxidant enzymes, like SOD, CAT, and GSH-Px [ 24 , 90 – 92 ] (Table 2 ). For example, EGCG from green tea significantly improved cell survival by activating the Nrf2 pathway and down-regulating pro-inflammatory factor expression in a cell model of H₂O₂-induced oxidative damage [ 60 ]. Furthermore, in vitro assays using breast cancer, kidney cells and endothelial cells have demonstrated the positive effects of tea polyphenols in inhibiting apoptosis and improving cellular energy metabolism [ 29 , 93 – 95 ]. Animal experiments further validated the role of tea polyphenols in modulating oxidative stress in vivo. Animal models commonly used in research include ischemia-reperfusion injury model, obesity model, neurotoxicity model and chemically induced tumour model. Molina et al. (2015) demonstrated in a high-fat diet-induced obese rat model that tea polyphenols significantly elevated SOD and GSH-Px activities and reduced malondialdehyde (MDA) levels through activation of the Nrf2 pathway [ 96 ]. In a Parkinson’s disease model, Mandel et al. (2004) found that EGCG reduced midbrain dopaminergic neuronal damage by inhibiting the production of ROS and NO, and decreased the expression of the lipid peroxidation marker 3-nitrotyrosine [ 41 , 97 ]. In liver injury research, fatty liver ischemia/reperfusion (I/R) model with methylmercury-induced oxidative damage to hepatocytes has been applied to study the mechanism [ 98 , 99 ].

Black tea At the cellular level, studies using rat intestinal epithelial cells (RIE), osteoarthritic chondrocytes, human malignant melanoma cells (A375), HepG2 cells and other in vitro models have demonstrated that black tea polyphenol treatment significantly reduces the intracellular level of ROS, and up-regulates Gpx4 to enhance antioxidant activity, while inhibiting the activation of NF-κB, MAPK, and other pro-inflammatory signaling pathways [ 24 , 90 – 92 ].The animal models studied mainly focus on tumour models. The anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities of black tea have been demonstrated through various experimental models (Table 3 ).

In-depth analysis of the mechanism Molecular mechanism studies need to delve into the dynamic regulation of epigenetic networks by polyphenols. It has now been shown that black tea theaflavins activate antioxidant gene expression by inhibiting DNA methylation transferase enzymes (DNMTs), or green tea EGCG reshapes redox balance by regulating miRNAs [ 153 – 156 ]. Also focusing on ferroptosis, a novel mode of cell death. To explore the molecular details of how theaflavins inhibit lipid peroxidation via the Nrf2/GPX4 pathway and the mechanism by which EGCG regulates mitochondrial autophagy and energy metabolism, and to elucidate how they resist oxidative damage by restoring mitochondrial membrane potential and ATP synthesis [ 156 – 159 ]. In addition, although many studies have revealed the roles of Nrf2, NF-κB and other signaling pathways, systematic studies are still needed to investigate the differential modulation of key signaling pathways such as Nrf2, NF-κB, MAPK and other key signaling pathways by black and green tea polyphenols, and to clarify their interactions in inflammatory-oxidative stress cascade responses. Future studies will unveil the synergistic antioxidant network of tea polyphenols and ultimately advance precision nutritional healthcare strategies for oxidative stress-related conditions. While, side-by-side studies controlling for black tea versus green tea dosage and formulation will distinguish between component-specific effects and differences due to administration. And the comparative studies should include multi-omics and biomarker profiling, pharmacokinetic and bioavailability analyses, and other biological parameters, like mitochondrial potential, ATP, autophagy, ferroptosis and so on. Such parallel studies make it possible to identify common targets for a wide range of antioxidant strategies and specific targets suitable for customised applications.

Conclusion This review innovatively highlights the different biologically active propensities of black and green tea under a direct comparative framework. With this comparative perspective, specific translational medicine opportunities can be identified and testable hypotheses can be formulated, making it clear that depending on the functional differences between black and green teas, specific formulations and deliveries may play a role in specific clinical contexts. Comparative studies reveal strategic differences: Catechin-rich green tea may respond to acute oxidative stress through potent direct scavenging effects and Nrf2-mediated activation of endogenous antioxidant systems, whereas black tea, which is rich in the fermentation products theaflavin and thearubigin, excels in mitigating the subsequent effects triggered by oxidative stress through potent inhibition of inflammation-associated signalling pathways as well as blocking apoptosis. The functional differences between black and green tea provide a mechanistic basis for the selective application of tea polyphenols according to specific pathological conditions. However, a significant translational gap persists between the reliable mechanistic evidence from in vitro and animal models and the divergent outcomes observed in human clinical trials. This disconnect primarily stems from three key challenges: low bioavailability, biphasic dose-response effects, and substantial inter-individual variations. Future research must therefore prioritise the following key areas: Development of advanced delivery systems (e.g. nano-encapsulation) to improve stability and bioavailability of tea polyphenols; Implement large-scale, tightly controlled human studies that incorporate nutrigenomic and metabolomic approaches to understand individual responses and explore personalised nutrition programmes; Strengthen research on black tea and its active ingredients to fully develop its potential; Deeper exploration of epigenetic regulation and new cell death pathways (e.g. ferroptosis) to fully elucidate mechanisms of action. By addressing these priorities, the field can advance personalised nutritional interventions that take advantage of the unique benefits of black and green tea tea polyphenols as an effective natural strategy for the prevention and management of oxidative stress-related diseases.

Methods Based on current literature, we focused on three miRNAs—miR-24, miR-21, and miR-155—previously linked to inflammation, senescence, and metabolic regulation. Capillary blood samples from a heterogeneous adult cohort were analyzed using quantitative PCR. ΔCt values were integrated into a composite “miRNA-3Age” model through multivariate regression analysis to estimate biological age. Associations between lifestyle variables (diet, exercise, stress, and smoking) and miRNA-based biological age were examined.

Results The miRNA-3Age model predicted biological age with moderate correlation to chronological age and revealed variability consistent with individual health profiles. Participants with favorable lifestyle factors (e.g., frequent consumption of fish, whole grains, and green tea; regular exercise) tended to exhibit lower miRNA-3Age estimates, whereas stress and smoking were associated with higher predicted biological age.

Materials and methods Cohort recruitment The study cohort, consisting of 1,151 subjects, was recruited between 2015 and 2023 through an Austrian company specializing in personalized nutrition. Data collection included dietary information, lifestyle factors, medical history, and behavioral characteristics, obtained through self-completed questionnaires. Additionally, whole blood samples were collected from each participant for analysis. To develop a broadly applicable aging marker for the general population, several exclusion criteria were established. These exclusion criteria encompassed individuals under 18 years of age and participants with specific medical conditions. The excluded conditions included arthritis, high blood pressure, diabetes mellitus type 1 and 2, gout, thyroid dysfunction and inflammatory gastrointestinal diseases. After applying these criteria, 539 participants qualified for the study, forming a “healthy” baseline population free from the specified conditions. MicroRNA selection Based on literature review, three microRNAs previously associated with aging processes were selected for analysis: miR-21, miR-24, and miR-155. MicroRNA-93 was used as a housekeeping reference for normalization. RNA extraction and qPCR Dried capillary blood samples were collected on Whatman protein saver cards using 18G safety lancets. DNA and RNA extraction was performed using a magnetic bead-based method over two days. On day one, blood card punches were incubated overnight in protease buffer using a ThermoMixer. On day two, extraction was completed using the KingFisher™ Duo Prime Magnetic Particle Processor and MagMax FFPE DNA/RNA Ultra kit (ThermoScientific™). The extracted RNA was then used for cDNA synthesis using the TaqMan™ Advanced miRNA cDNA Synthesis kit and a SimpliAmp™ Thermal Cycler. The resulting amplified cDNA was diluted 1:10. Individual mastermixes were prepared for each microRNA target, combining the specific miRNA assay, nuclease-free water, and TaqMan™ Fast Advanced Master Mix for qPCR. The mastermixes were distributed into a 96-well plate, followed by the addition of sample cDNA to corresponding wells. Each run included a negative control containing only nuclease-free water. Quantitative PCR was performed using the QuantStudio™ 3 Real-time PCR System. Statistical analysis Statistical analysis was performed on qPCR data using Applied Biosystems qPCR analysis modules (Thermo Scientific™). The relative expression levels were calculated by subtracting the mean Ct value of the housekeeping microRNA-93 from the mean Ct values of the target microRNAs within each sample (ΔCt method). While statistical methods are briefly outlined here, a comprehensive description of the statistical models and their theoretical background will be presented in a separate paper currently in preparation. To ensure robust validation, the dataset was randomly split into training and testing subsets using an 80/20 ratio, resulting in 433 and 106 subjects, respectively. For comparing clinical and lifestyle variables between groups, appropriate statistical tests were applied based on data distribution: Student's t -test for normally distributed metric variables, Mann–Whitney U test for non-normally distributed metric variables, and Chi-square test for categorical variables. A multiple linear regression model was developed to estimate biological age, using chronological age as the reference. This age estimator will later be called miRNA-3Age. Model performance was evaluated using mean absolute error (MAE), the coefficient of determination ( R 2 ), and the median absolute error (MedAE). To identify biological aging patterns, we calculated an age deviation index (BA-CA) as the difference between biological age (BA) and chronological age (CA). Negative values indicate a biologically younger profile, while positive values suggest accelerated aging. The relationships between the age estimator and various lifestyle and dietary characteristics were assessed using logistic regression, with odds ratios (OR) and 95% confidence intervals (CI) calculated to quantify the likelihood of being biologically younger. All statistical analyses were performed using RStudio, with statistical significance set at p ≤ 0.05.
