The heterogeneity in signaling pathways and cellular responses to EGCG suggests that its efficacy may vary and warrants further investigation to optimize its use in clinical settings. In addition, although EGCG has shown low toxicity and high efficacy in preclinical studies, its effects in human trials are warranted to confirm its safety and efficacy in the treatment of vulvar cancer ( 81 , 82 ).

7 Endometriosis and EGCG relationship EGCG, exert therapeutic effects over endometriosis mostly through anti-angiogenic, anti-fibrotic, anti-proliferative, and proapoptotic mechanisms. 7.1 EGCG mechanisms and pathways in endometriosis Endometriosis is a chronic gynecological disorder characterized by the growth of endometrial tissue outside the uterus, leading to pain and infertility. Green tea and its major catechin, EGCG, Pro-EGCG, may affect endometriosis through multiple molecular pathways through various biological mechanisms ( 83–87 ). EGCG and Pro-EGCG inhibit the formation of new blood vessels (angiogenesis), which is essential for endometriotic lesion growth. EGCG acts primarily by downregulating VEGF and related pathways, while Pro-EGCG has been shown to downregulate PX domain containing serine/threonine kinase (PXK)-mediated EGF expression by binding to PXK, inhibit angiogenesis via HIF-1α, and inhibit angiogenesis more potently by suppressing EGF-mediated signaling via the HIF-1α/VEGF pathway. This mechanism is crucial during the growth and development of endometriosis, as angiogenesis is an important pathophysiological process in the disease ( 83 , 88 ). Both compounds reduce lesion size and vascularization in animal models ( 11 , 83 , 84 ). EGCG suppresses the proliferation of endometrial cells and induces apoptosis (programmed cell death) in endometriotic tissue, contributing to lesion regression ( 11 , 28 , 29 , 84 ). EGCG and Pro-EGCG regulate immune cell interactions, specifically by reducing dysfunctional macrophages and B cells associated with endometriosis. They target proteins such as metadherin (MTDH) and PXK, alter downstream effectors (e.g., BLK, EGF, MYC, AKT), and reduce immune cell infiltration in lesions ( 83 , 85 ). EGCG and Pro-EGCG interact with DNMTs and HDACs, influencing epigenetic modifications that regulate genes involved in angiogenesis and cell proliferation ( 29 , 46 , 84 , 85 ). They also demonstrate biological activities that contribute to reduced oxidative stress and inflammation in endometriotic tissue ( 11 , 28 , 84 , 85 ). Key proteins are MTDH, PXK, nicotinamide mononucleotide adenylyltransferase 1 (NMNAT1), nicotinamide mononucleotide adenylyltransferase 3 (NMNAT3), and signaling pathways are Akt/PI3K, EGF/Akt1/HIF-1α/VEGF, ERK, p38 MAPK. In addition, immune modulation occurs by down-regulating macrophage and B cell activity and reducing inflammatory gene expression ( 83 , 86 ). Pro-EGCG’s inhibition of angiogenesis via HIF-1α is important because this pathway is involved in the response to hypoxia, a condition often found in endometriotic lesions ( 86 , 88 ). EGCG and Pro-EGCG down-regulate VEGF, a critical factor in angiogenesis. The effects of EGCG on the VEGF pathway are also mediated through the TGF-β1 classical-SMAD pathway and the 67LR-mediated CREB pathway, which are involved in reducing ovarian inflammation and angiogenesis ( 62 ). EGCG has been shown to reduce oxidative stress and inflammation via the sirtuin 1 / nod-like receptor protein 3 (SIRT1/NLRP3) pathway, which may contribute to its protective effects against endometriosis ( 87 ). Animal studies consistently show that EGCG and Pro-EGCG reduce lesion size, inhibit angiogenesis, and promote apoptosis in endometriosis models ( 11 , 84 ). Pro-EGCG shows higher bioavailability and efficacy compared to EGCG, making it a promising candidate for future therapies ( 11 , 85 , 86 ). Neither EGCG nor Pro-EGCG adversely affect ovarian follicles or uterine glands in animal models ( 11 ). Most evidence comes from preclinical studies; robust clinical studies are needed to confirm the therapeutic benefits and safety of EGCG and Pro-EGCG in humans ( 28 , 29 , 85 ). Although multiple mechanisms have been identified, further research is needed to fully understand the interaction between immune modulation, angiogenesis, and oxidative stress in the treatment of endometriosis ( 28 , 85 , 86 ). In conclusion, EGCG and pro-EGCG in green tea inhibit endometriosis progression by targeting angiogenesis, immune cell regulation, apoptosis, and oxidative stress through different molecular pathways. Pro-EGCG provides superior efficacy and bioavailability. These effects are mediated through specific molecular targets and pathways. Most of the evidence comes from preclinical models and clinical validation in humans is needed.

sections.section_title: EGCG mechanisms and pathways in endometriosis
sections.section_text: 7.1 EGCG mechanisms and pathways in endometriosis Endometriosis is a chronic gynecological disorder characterized by the growth of endometrial tissue outside the uterus, leading to pain and infertility. Green tea and its major catechin, EGCG, Pro-EGCG, may affect endometriosis through multiple molecular pathways through various biological mechanisms ( 83–87 ). EGCG and Pro-EGCG inhibit the formation of new blood vessels (angiogenesis), which is essential for endometriotic lesion growth. EGCG acts primarily by downregulating VEGF and related pathways, while Pro-EGCG has been shown to downregulate PX domain containing serine/threonine kinase (PXK)-mediated EGF expression by binding to PXK, inhibit angiogenesis via HIF-1α, and inhibit angiogenesis more potently by suppressing EGF-mediated signaling via the HIF-1α/VEGF pathway. This mechanism is crucial during the growth and development of endometriosis, as angiogenesis is an important pathophysiological process in the disease ( 83 , 88 ). Both compounds reduce lesion size and vascularization in animal models ( 11 , 83 , 84 ). EGCG suppresses the proliferation of endometrial cells and induces apoptosis (programmed cell death) in endometriotic tissue, contributing to lesion regression ( 11 , 28 , 29 , 84 ). EGCG and Pro-EGCG regulate immune cell interactions, specifically by reducing dysfunctional macrophages and B cells associated with endometriosis. They target proteins such as metadherin (MTDH) and PXK, alter downstream effectors (e.g., BLK, EGF, MYC, AKT), and reduce immune cell infiltration in lesions ( 83 , 85 ). EGCG and Pro-EGCG interact with DNMTs and HDACs, influencing epigenetic modifications that regulate genes involved in angiogenesis and cell proliferation ( 29 , 46 , 84 , 85 ). They also demonstrate biological activities that contribute to reduced oxidative stress and inflammation in endometriotic tissue ( 11 , 28 , 84 , 85 ). Key proteins are MTDH, PXK, nicotinamide mononucleotide adenylyltransferase 1 (NMNAT1), nicotinamide mononucleotide adenylyltransferase 3 (NMNAT3), and signaling pathways are Akt/PI3K, EGF/Akt1/HIF-1α/VEGF, ERK, p38 MAPK. In addition, immune modulation occurs by down-regulating macrophage and B cell activity and reducing inflammatory gene expression ( 83 , 86 ). Pro-EGCG’s inhibition of angiogenesis via HIF-1α is important because this pathway is involved in the response to hypoxia, a condition often found in endometriotic lesions ( 86 , 88 ). EGCG and Pro-EGCG down-regulate VEGF, a critical factor in angiogenesis. The effects of EGCG on the VEGF pathway are also mediated through the TGF-β1 classical-SMAD pathway and the 67LR-mediated CREB pathway, which are involved in reducing ovarian inflammation and angiogenesis ( 62 ). EGCG has been shown to reduce oxidative stress and inflammation via the sirtuin 1 / nod-like receptor protein 3 (SIRT1/NLRP3) pathway, which may contribute to its protective effects against endometriosis ( 87 ). Animal studies consistently show that EGCG and Pro-EGCG reduce lesion size, inhibit angiogenesis, and promote apoptosis in endometriosis models ( 11 , 84 ). Pro-EGCG shows higher bioavailability and efficacy compared to EGCG, making it a promising candidate for future therapies ( 11 , 85 , 86 ). Neither EGCG nor Pro-EGCG adversely affect ovarian follicles or uterine glands in animal models ( 11 ). Most evidence comes from preclinical studies; robust clinical studies are needed to confirm the therapeutic benefits and safety of EGCG and Pro-EGCG in humans ( 28 , 29 , 85 ). Although multiple mechanisms have been identified, further research is needed to fully understand the interaction between immune modulation, angiogenesis, and oxidative stress in the treatment of endometriosis ( 28 , 85 , 86 ). In conclusion, EGCG and pro-EGCG in green tea inhibit endometriosis progression by targeting angiogenesis, immune cell regulation, apoptosis, and oxidative stress through different molecular pathways. Pro-EGCG provides superior efficacy and bioavailability. These effects are mediated through specific molecular targets and pathways. Most of the evidence comes from preclinical models and clinical validation in humans is needed.

sections.section_title: Mechanisms of action and signaling pathways of green tea and EGCG in PCOS
sections.section_text: 8.1 Mechanisms of action and signaling pathways of green tea and EGCG in PCOS EGCG has been shown to affect hormone levels in PCOS. In human studies, green tea extract (GTE) has been associated with weight loss and a reduction in testosterone levels, which are often elevated in PCOS patients ( 89 , 90 ). Green tea extract has been shown to decrease testosterone and luteinizing hormone (LH) levels in animal models, while increasing follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) and progesterone. This hormonal modulation may help restore ovarian function and improve PCOS symptoms ( 89 , 90 ). EGCG acts as a potent antioxidant that reduces oxidative stress and inflammation, which are central to PCOS pathology. These effects are mediated through modulation of cellular pathways that control inflammation and oxidative damage ( 7 , 11 , 29 , 90 , 91 ). It improves mitochondrial function, which may be beneficial in reducing oxidative damage associated with PCOS by strengthening antioxidant defenses ( 7 ). The anti-inflammatory effects of EGCG are mediated through the suppression of inflammatory cytokines such as TNF-a and IL-6. However, these effects have been observed more clearly in animal models ( 7 , 90 ). EGCG and green tea supplementation may lower fasting insulin, improve insulin resistance, and address underlying metabolic issues in PCOS by reducing body weight and body fat percentage ( 89–92 ). EGCG affects various signaling pathways related to PCOS. It up-regulates the expression of StAR and increases progesterone production in granulosa cells via the 67-kDa laminin receptor-mediated CREB signaling pathway ( 93 ). In addition, EGCG inhibits the VEGF pathway, which plays a role in angiogenesis and inflammation, via the TGF-β1 classical-SMAD pathway and the 67LR-mediated CREB pathway. This inhibition can reduce ovarian inflammation and hyperstimulation common in PCOS ( 62 ).

sections.section_title: Safe intake level and toxicity of catechins
sections.section_text: 9 Safe intake level and toxicity of catechins While moderate levels of catechin intake from traditional tea consumption are generally considered safe, higher doses, especially from supplements, raise concerns about liver toxicity. Daily EGCG intake from green tea infusions generally ranges from 90 to 300 mg and is considered safe for most people. Even high-level consumers (up to 866 mg per day) rarely experience adverse effects, although rare cases of idiosyncratic liver injury have been reported ( 100 , 101 ). For concentrated green tea extracts or supplements, a tolerable upper intake level of 300 mg EGCG per day is recommended to provide a safety margin against liver toxicity. Clinical studies show no liver effects below 600 mg/day, but doses of 800 mg or more per day have been associated with increases in liver enzymes, indicating potential harm ( 100–102 ). A safe observed level for beverage forms is up to 704 mg EGCG per day, but for solid bolus doses, 338 mg EGCG per day is considered safe ( 103 ). The main risk at high intakes is liver toxicity, especially with supplements or large single doses ( 100–102 ). Rare cases of liver injury may occur even at lower intakes due to individual sensitivity. Typical dietary catechin intake from tea and food is much lower than supplemental doses and is not associated with adverse effects. Up to 300 mg/day of EGCG from tea is generally considered safe for healthy adults. This equals about 3–5 cups of brewed green tea per day, depending on strength and variety. One cup of green tea contains 25–86 mg EGCG. EFSA has reported that intakes of >800 mg/day are not safe and present a risk. Excessive consumption can lead to elevated liver enzymes, with the risk being greater when EGCG is taken on an empty stomach or in concentrated supplement form ( 104 ). 9.1 Limitations This review is limited by the predominance of observational studies, often relying on food frequency questionnaires and self-reported data, which are subject to recall bias and measurement error. The paucity of high-quality case–control and cohort studies limits causal inference. Significant heterogeneity in study design, methodology, and sample size further complicates cross-study comparisons. Moreover, the direct molecular link between dietary intake and gynecologic disease mechanisms remains insufficiently defined. Robust randomized trials are urgently needed to establish definitive associations and clarify the therapeutic potential of catechins and related compounds in female health.

9.1 Limitations This review is limited by the predominance of observational studies, often relying on food frequency questionnaires and self-reported data, which are subject to recall bias and measurement error. The paucity of high-quality case–control and cohort studies limits causal inference. Significant heterogeneity in study design, methodology, and sample size further complicates cross-study comparisons. Moreover, the direct molecular link between dietary intake and gynecologic disease mechanisms remains insufficiently defined. Robust randomized trials are urgently needed to establish definitive associations and clarify the therapeutic potential of catechins and related compounds in female health.

10 Conclusion and future perspective Catechins, especially EGCG, demonstrate promising therapeutic potential in gynecologic conditions such as endometrial, ovarian, cervical, and vulvar cancers, as well as PCOS and endometriosis. Their effects are mediated through well-characterized mechanisms including antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antiproliferative, and epigenetic pathways. However, much of the available evidence is preclinical, which limits direct clinical translation. Safety concerns also arise with high-dose or concentrated extract use. Future well-designed human trials are essential to clarify efficacy, optimize dosing, and ensure safety. With appropriate validation, catechins may offer a safe, adjunctive approach to gynecologic care, particularly within personalized and integrative treatment frameworks. Although some studies report that EGCG exhibits anti-inflammatory, antiproliferative, and anti-angiogenic effects in animal and cell models, these findings remain heterogeneous across gynecologic conditions. Outcomes vary depending on dose, study design, and cell line, and results derived from metastatic models or hormone-dependent pathways are not always consistent. To clarify these conflicting findings, well-designed clinical studies using standardized protocols are needed. Future research should prioritize well-designed randomized controlled trials and mechanistic studies to clarify the independent effects of catechins and broader dietary patterns on gynecologic disease risk and progression. Current evidence, largely derived from observational studies, remains vulnerable to confounding and cannot establish causality. Integrating dietary factors into gynecologic disease prevention and management demands a multidisciplinary approach that accounts for genetic variability, age, nutritional status, polyphenol intake, environmental exposures, and regional dietary differences. Consideration of factors such as green tea preparation methods, bioavailability, and individual sensitivities (e.g., allergies or hepatotoxicity risk) is essential for developing evidence-based guidelines. Advancing this field will depend on harmonized protocols and diverse population studies to enable the safe and personalized application of dietary strategies particularly catechins as adjuncts in gynecologic care.

Introduction Tea, derived from the leaves of Camellia sinensis , is one of the most consumed beverages worldwide and is valued for its cultural significance, sensory qualities, and purported health benefits. Its consumption spans centuries, with historical records tracing its origins to ancient China around 2700 BCE 1 . Tea is categorized into several types—green, black, white, and oolong–based on the processing methods, which influence its chemical composition and bioactive properties 2 . These types differ in their levels of oxidation and fermentation, which affect the presence of minerals, antioxidants, and phytochemicals. The mineral content of tea, including essential elements such as potassium, calcium, and magnesium, contributes to its nutritional profile, whereas trace elements such as zinc and iron play roles in human health 3 . Antioxidants, particularly polyphenols such as catechins, are credited with the health-promoting effects of tea, including anti-carcinogenic and anti-inflammatory properties 4 . The diverse chemical makeup of tea makes it a subject of scientific interest, as its components may vary based on cultivation practices, soil composition, and processing techniques 5 . The antioxidant activity of tea is principally due to its polyphenolic constituents, which counteract free radicals and oxidative stress within biological systems 6 . Studies on green tea have focused mostly on catechins, particularly epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG), while black tea contains more theaflavins and thearubigins due to enzymatic oxidation during processing 7 . These constituents have been associated with chemoprevention of cardiovascular ailments, cancer, and neurodegenerative diseases 8 . In addition to antioxidants, tea has a broad range of phytochemicals, including flavonoids and alkaloids, such as caffeine and other compounds with therapeutic value, and plant-derived substances that enhance its fragrance 9 . Gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) has emerged as a powerful tool for identifying these phytochemicals, providing detailed profiles of volatile and semi-volatile compounds that define the sensory and bioactive characteristics 10 . However, the interplay between minerals, antioxidants, and phytochemicals in tea remains underexplored, particularly regarding how these components collectively contribute to health benefits 11 . The environment and agricultural practices, including soil pH, geographic location, and farming techniques such as soil nutrition, affect the minerals in tea that are absorbed during cultivation 12 . For example, the presence of trace elements in soils might enhance the nutritional value and flavor of tea 13 . Although a great deal has been done to study antioxidants and phytochemicals, the mineral content of tea is less well-known, particularly regarding its antioxidant action and phytochemical profile 14 . The health benefits of tea may be improved by the synergistic effects of these components; however, to date, few studies have analyzed tea minerals, antioxidant capacity, and phytochemicals 15 . Understanding these relationships is critical because they may influence the role of tea in dietary nutrition and disease prevention. Relatively few studies have been conducted in Saudi Arabia, focusing on the comprehensive analysis of mineral content, antioxidant capacity, and the phytochemical profile of black tea employing cutting-edge methods such as GC-MS, with a focus on the bioactive components of black tea. This understanding gap highlights the role that these features play in the health benefits and sensory attributes of tea. This analysis aimed to fill this gap by assessing the mineral profile and antioxidant properties of various types of tea, as well as their phytochemical constituents. This work is important from the standpoint of defining nutrition that determines their relationships and value and therapeutic applications of tea, as well as the relevant recommendations on quality control for tea production. This study combined GC-MS-based phytochemical analysis with mineral content and antioxidant activity evaluation to comprehensively assess the chemical constituents of tea. The primary goal of this study was to systematically investigate the mineral content, antioxidant activity, and phytochemical constituents of different tea samples ( N = 20) to determine their relationships and collective implications for health and industrial use.

Materials and methods Samples Twenty commercial tea brands were selected based on their widespread availability and popularity in the Saudi Arabian market. The selection aimed to represent a diverse range of geographic origins, including Sri Lanka, India, the United Arab Emirates, Saudi Arabia, and the United Kingdom, in order to capture regional variability in tea composition. These brands were not selected randomly but were chosen to reflect consumer-accessible products from different international source. To protect confidentiality and not reveal the trade names of the tea brands, they were referred to as tea brands throughout the document. One gram of powdered tea was extracted with 10 mL of methanol, sonicated for 2 h, filtered using a 0.45 μm syringe filter, and analyzed. To account for within-brand variability, three different batches were obtained for each of the 20 commercial tea brands, and all analyses were conducted on pooled or representative samples derived from these batches. Mineral analysis by ICP-MS Inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) was used to map the mineral content of the 20 tea samples. The brands were imported into Saudi Arabia and later prepared for analysis. Each tea sample (approximately 0.5 g) was subjected to acid digestion, which allowed preparation for further examination. The samples were processed using a microwave digestion system that included a Teflon vessel and concentrated nitric acid (65% w/w, analytical grade) with a volume of 10 mL. The digestion program comprised a 15-minute ramp to 180 °C, followed by a 25-minute hold at 180 °C, and a cooling phase to room temperature. The processed samples were diluted to 50 mL with ultrapure-water (18.2 MΩ·cm) to achieve an acid concentration of approximately 2% (v/v). The diluted tea digests were examined using ICP-MS (Agilent 7800, Agilent Technologies, USA) equipped with a quadrupole mass analyzer and a collision/reaction cell to reduce polyatomic interferences. Instrument calibration was conducted using multi-element standards (TraceCERT, Sigma-Aldrich) that included the elements of interest: Ag, Al, As, Ba, Be, Cu, Mn, Mo, Cd, Cr, Ni, Pb, Sb, Se, and Zn. The calibration solutions were prepared in a 2% nitric acid (HNO₃) matrix with concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 100 µg/L. An internal standard solution containing indium (In) and rhodium (Rh), each at 10 µg/L, was introduced online to compensate for the matrix effects and instrumental drift. The operational parameters included a plasma power setting of 1550 W, argon carrier gas flow rate of one L/min, and helium collision gas flow rate of 4.5 mL/min for interference suppression. Each sample was investigated in triplicate, and blank samples containing 2% HNO₃ were run to assess background contamination. Quality assurance was maintained by including certified reference standards ( NIST SRM 1515 , Apple Leaves ) in the analysis, achieving recovery rates between 95% and 105% for all the elements. The detection limits (LOD) and quantification limits (LOQ) were determined as three and ten times the standard deviation of the blank readings, respectively, ensuring accurate trace element measurements. Recovery experiments were performed by spiking known concentrations of certified multi-element standards (including Pb, Cd, Mn, Zn, Fe, and Cu) into selected tea samples prior to digestion. Each spiked sample was analyzed in triplicate, and recoveries were calculated by comparing the measured concentration to the expected value. This protocol ensured accuracy and reliability of the ICP-MS results. Data acquisition and processing were performed using Agilent MassHunter software, with mineral concentrations expressed in mg/kg of dry tea material. Statistical analysis was applied to assess elemental variations across different tea types and origins, and the findings were subsequently correlated with antioxidant capacity and phytochemical composition. GC-MS analysis Phytochemical profiling of tea samples was conducted using a Thermo Scientific ISQ 7610 Single Quadrupole GC-MS system integrated with a TRACE 1610 gas chromatograph. One gram of powdered tea was extracted with 10 mL of methanol, sonicated for 2 h, filtered using a 0.45 μm syringe filter, and analyzed. A 1 µL aliquot was injected in split mode (1:20) into a TraceGOLD TG-5MS column (30 m × 0.25 mm I.D., 0.25 μm film thickness) using helium as the carrier gas at a flow rate of 1.0 mL/min. The temperature of the oven was programmed from 50 °C (held for 2 min) to 150 °C at 10 °C/min and then to 280 °C at 5 °C/min (held for 5 min). Mass spectral data were acquired in the electron ionization (EI) mode at 70 eV over an m/z range of 50–550. Phytochemicals were identified by matching spectra against the NIST 2017 and in-house libraries, with a minimum similarity threshold of 80%, and the results were expressed as relative peak areas (% of the total ion chromatogram). Analyses were performed in triplicate, including s

Samples Twenty commercial tea brands were selected based on their widespread availability and popularity in the Saudi Arabian market. The selection aimed to represent a diverse range of geographic origins, including Sri Lanka, India, the United Arab Emirates, Saudi Arabia, and the United Kingdom, in order to capture regional variability in tea composition. These brands were not selected randomly but were chosen to reflect consumer-accessible products from different international source. To protect confidentiality and not reveal the trade names of the tea brands, they were referred to as tea brands throughout the document. One gram of powdered tea was extracted with 10 mL of methanol, sonicated for 2 h, filtered using a 0.45 μm syringe filter, and analyzed. To account for within-brand variability, three different batches were obtained for each of the 20 commercial tea brands, and all analyses were conducted on pooled or representative samples derived from these batches.

Mineral analysis by ICP-MS Inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) was used to map the mineral content of the 20 tea samples. The brands were imported into Saudi Arabia and later prepared for analysis. Each tea sample (approximately 0.5 g) was subjected to acid digestion, which allowed preparation for further examination. The samples were processed using a microwave digestion system that included a Teflon vessel and concentrated nitric acid (65% w/w, analytical grade) with a volume of 10 mL. The digestion program comprised a 15-minute ramp to 180 °C, followed by a 25-minute hold at 180 °C, and a cooling phase to room temperature. The processed samples were diluted to 50 mL with ultrapure-water (18.2 MΩ·cm) to achieve an acid concentration of approximately 2% (v/v). The diluted tea digests were examined using ICP-MS (Agilent 7800, Agilent Technologies, USA) equipped with a quadrupole mass analyzer and a collision/reaction cell to reduce polyatomic interferences. Instrument calibration was conducted using multi-element standards (TraceCERT, Sigma-Aldrich) that included the elements of interest: Ag, Al, As, Ba, Be, Cu, Mn, Mo, Cd, Cr, Ni, Pb, Sb, Se, and Zn. The calibration solutions were prepared in a 2% nitric acid (HNO₃) matrix with concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 100 µg/L. An internal standard solution containing indium (In) and rhodium (Rh), each at 10 µg/L, was introduced online to compensate for the matrix effects and instrumental drift. The operational parameters included a plasma power setting of 1550 W, argon carrier gas flow rate of one L/min, and helium collision gas flow rate of 4.5 mL/min for interference suppression. Each sample was investigated in triplicate, and blank samples containing 2% HNO₃ were run to assess background contamination. Quality assurance was maintained by including certified reference standards ( NIST SRM 1515 , Apple Leaves ) in the analysis, achieving recovery rates between 95% and 105% for all the elements. The detection limits (LOD) and quantification limits (LOQ) were determined as three and ten times the standard deviation of the blank readings, respectively, ensuring accurate trace element measurements. Recovery experiments were performed by spiking known concentrations of certified multi-element standards (including Pb, Cd, Mn, Zn, Fe, and Cu) into selected tea samples prior to digestion. Each spiked sample was analyzed in triplicate, and recoveries were calculated by comparing the measured concentration to the expected value. This protocol ensured accuracy and reliability of the ICP-MS results. Data acquisition and processing were performed using Agilent MassHunter software, with mineral concentrations expressed in mg/kg of dry tea material. Statistical analysis was applied to assess elemental variations across different tea types and origins, and the findings were subsequently correlated with antioxidant capacity and phytochemical composition.

GC-MS analysis Phytochemical profiling of tea samples was conducted using a Thermo Scientific ISQ 7610 Single Quadrupole GC-MS system integrated with a TRACE 1610 gas chromatograph. One gram of powdered tea was extracted with 10 mL of methanol, sonicated for 2 h, filtered using a 0.45 μm syringe filter, and analyzed. A 1 µL aliquot was injected in split mode (1:20) into a TraceGOLD TG-5MS column (30 m × 0.25 mm I.D., 0.25 μm film thickness) using helium as the carrier gas at a flow rate of 1.0 mL/min. The temperature of the oven was programmed from 50 °C (held for 2 min) to 150 °C at 10 °C/min and then to 280 °C at 5 °C/min (held for 5 min). Mass spectral data were acquired in the electron ionization (EI) mode at 70 eV over an m/z range of 50–550. Phytochemicals were identified by matching spectra against the NIST 2017 and in-house libraries, with a minimum similarity threshold of 80%, and the results were expressed as relative peak areas (% of the total ion chromatogram). Analyses were performed in triplicate, including solvent blanks, for quality assurance. The limits of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) were determined based on signal-to-noise ratios of 3:1 and 10:1, respectively. The resulting data were used to compare phytochemical profiles among tea types and origins, and were further correlated with mineral and antioxidant contents 16 . Mass spectra and chromatograms for identified compounds are provided in the Supplementary Material (Table S1 ) to facilitate reproducibility and serve as a reference for future studies.

DPPH radical scavenging assay Antioxidant activity was evaluated using the DPPH assay 17 . One gram of powdered tea was extracted with 10 mL methanol, sonicated for 2 h, filtered using a 0.45 μm syringe filter, and analyzed. A 0.1 mM DPPH methanolic solution was used, and 0.1 mL of tea infusion was mixed with 3.9 mL of DPPH solution and left in the dark for 30 min. The absorbance was measured at 517 nm using methanol as the blank. A control using deionized water was used to establish the baseline absorbance.

Total phenolic content (TPC) determination The Folin-Ciocalteu method was used to measure the total phenolic content (TPC) 18 . One gram of powdered tea was extracted with 10 mL of methanol, sonicated for 2 h, filtered using a 0.45 μm syringe filter, and analyzed. For TPC analysis, 0.5 mL of each extract was mixed with 2.5 mL of 10% Folin-Ciocalteu reagent (Sigma-Aldrich) and 2 mL of 7.5% sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃) solution in a test tube. The mixture was thoroughly vortexed and allowed to react in the dark at ambient temperature for 60 min. Colorimetric absorbance was recorded at 765 nm using a Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer, with deionized water serving as the blank. Gallic acid was used as the calibration standard at concentrations ranging from 0 to 100 mg/L. The results are expressed as milligrams of gallic acid equivalent (GAE) per gram of dry tea (mg GAE/g). All analyses were performed in triplicate, and quality assurance included blank and gallic acid standards for each batch. This procedure is based on the method described by Singleton and Rossi (1965). Data from TPC and DPPH analyses were statistically evaluated to identify differences among tea types and sources and to examine associations with mineral and phytochemical content.

Multivariate analysis Chemometric analyses were performed using SPSS (Version 26.0, IBM Corp.). Principal Component Analysis (PCA) was applied to estimate dimensionality and recognize patterns with standardized data to ensure equal variance. Varimax rotation was used to enhance the interpretability of principal components. Hierarchical Cluster Analysis (HCA) was conducted using Ward’s method with Euclidean distances to group samples based on chemical similarities. Triplicate data were averaged, and the results were visualized through dendrograms and score plots to assess variations across tea types and origins, correlated with ICP-MS.

Correlogram Spearman correlation analysis among the measured elements revealed several statistically significant relationships, as illustrated in the correlogram (Fig. 1 ). Notably, Aluminium (Al) demonstrated strong positive correlations with Barium (Ba) (ρ = 0.79, p < 0.01), Nickel (Ni) (ρ = 0.63, p < 0.01), and (Zn) (ρ = 0.58, p < 0.01), and a moderate inverse correlation with Selenium (Se) (ρ=-0.50, p < 0.05). Arsenic (As) was positively associated with Ba (ρ = 0.48, p < 0.05) and Zn (ρ = 0.60, p < 0.01), but showed a strong negative association with Se (ρ=-0.69, p < 0.01). Ba also had a strong positive association with Zn (ρ = 0.73, p < 0.01) and a strong negative correlation with Se (ρ=-0.73, p < 0.01). Cadmium (Cd) was significantly and positively correlated with Lead (Pb) (ρ = 0.69, p < 0.01). Chromium (Cr) showed a strong correlation with Ni (ρ = 0.60, p < 0.01) and a moderate correlation with Manganese (Mn) (ρ = 0.53, p < 0.05). Copper (Cu) had a notable positive correlation with Zn (ρ = 0.64, p < 0.01). Mn was positively associated with Be (ρ = 0.53, P < 0.05) and Pb (ρ = 0.60, P < 0.01). Se exhibited a strong negative correlation with As (ρ=-0.69, p < 0.01), Ba (ρ=-0.73, p < 0.01), and Zn (ρ=-0.70, p < 0.01). Overall, Zn demonstrated multiple strong correlations, positively with Al, As, Ba, and Cu and negatively with Se. These findings suggest the existence of shared geochemical behaviors or sources among specific elements, supporting the multivariate relationships shown in Fig. 1 . Fig. 1 Correlogram of Elemental Concentrations. The correlogram displays the Pearson correlation coefficients between elemental concentrations in the tea samples, using a color scale where dark blue indicates strong positive correlations (+ 1), white represents no correlation (0), and dark red indicates strong negative correlations (–1). The matrix is symmetrical, and each colored square represents the strength and direction of the correlation between a pair of elements (e.g., Cu vs. Zn). Element symbols are shown for clarity, while numerical values have been removed for a cleaner visual representation. Correlation strength can be interpreted as follows: values with an absolute correlation coefficient (|r|) of 0.7 or higher indicate strong correlation, values between 0.4 and 0.69 suggest moderate correlation, and values below 0.4 indicate weak correlation. This visualization aids in identifying co-occurrence patterns and potential associations among trace elements in the tea samples.

PCA with varimax rotation A factor analysis was conducted using PCA with Varimax rotation to explore the underlying structure of the elemental dataset (Fig. 2 ). The Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin (KMO) measure of sampling adequacy was 0.481, indicating a mediocre level of adequacy for factor analysis contamination-related associations. Bartlett’s Test of Sphericity showed statistical significance (χ²=163.878, df = 78, p < 0.001), supporting the suitability of the present data for factor analysis. Four principal components were extracted based on eigenvalues greater than one, cumulatively explaining 77.61% of the total variance. After rotation, component 1 accounted for 26.31% of the variance, followed by component 2 (19.37%), component 3 (16.99%), and component 4 (14.93%). The rotated component matrix revealed distinct groupings of elements. Component 1 was primarily associated with Al (0.929), Zn (0.798), Ba (0.734), Cu (0.683), and Ni (0.649), suggesting geochemical or anthropogenic source patterns. Component 2 featured high loadings for Pb (0.869), Cd (0.654), Ni (0.682), Cr (0.627), and Ag (0.613), possibly indicating contamination related associations. Component 3 is dominated by Be (0.793) and Mn (0.727), potentially representing natural or lithogenic origins. Component 4 loaded heavily on Se (0.839) and negatively on As (-0.791), suggesting contrasting behaviors or sources between these elements. These multidimensional relationships are visually represented in the 3D rotated component plot (Fig. 2 ), which illustrates the spatial separation and clustering of elements across the first three principal components. Fig. 2 Rotated component plot of elemental concentrations based on principal component analysis (PCA). This 3D plot illustrates the spatial distribution of elements across the first three rotated principal components. The clustering of elements reflects shared variance patterns, indicating possible common sources or geochemical behavior. Elements such as Al, Zn, Cu, and Ba group closely along Component 1, while Pb, Cd, Cr, and Ag align along Component 2, suggesting anthropogenic influences. Se and As show contrasting loadings, highlighting their unique contribution to Component 3.

DPPH radical scavenging assay results The antioxidant capacity of the tea samples was assessed using the DPPH radical scavenging assay (Table 4 ). The absorbance of the control was 1.5644 ± 0.067. The sample absorbance ranged from 0.926 ± 0.048 (sample 5) to 1.420 ± 0.067 (sample 16), with corresponding DPPH inhibition percentages varying from 11 ± 0.4% (sample 16) to 69 ± 2.8% (sample 5). High inhibition was observed in samples 5 (69 ± 2.8%), 6 (65 ± 2.6%), 1 (64 ± 2.6%), 7 (63 ± 2.8%), 4 (62 ± 2.4%), 11 (56 ± 2.8%), and 18 (56 ± 1.9%), while lower inhibition was noted in samples 15 (18 ± 0.5%), 20 (18 ± 0.8%), 17 (14 ± 0.6%), and 16 (11 ± 0.4%). The other samples showed moderate inhibition, including samples 2 (43 ± 1.9%), 9 (42 ± 1.8%), 3 (34 ± 1.4%), 12 (33 ± 1.2%), 8 (32 ± 1.6%), 14 (32 ± 1.4%), 19 (31 ± 1.7%), 13 (24 ± 1.1%), and 10 (21 ± 1.1%).

Total phenolic content (TPC) results TPC values (Table 4 ) ranged from 256.22 to 361.84 mg gallic acid equivalent (GAE) per mL of tea infusion.
