2.1. Study Design and Participants This study was a double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled, parallel-group trial entitled “Intervention Study for Reduction of Visceral Fat by Tea Catechins and Inulin”. This trial was registered in the Japan Registry of Clinical Trials (jRCT; trial ID: jRCTs021230004) on 16 May 2023 ( https://jrct.mhlw.go.jp/en-latest-detail/jRCTs021230004 , accessed on 2 March 2026). Japanese men and women aged 20–75 years were recruited from the community. Key inclusion criteria were VFA ≥ 80 cm 2 , measured by bioelectrical impedance analysis, and BMI ≥ 23 kg/m 2 at baseline. Major exclusion criteria included a change in body weight of ±2 kg or more during the 3 months prior to enrollment, serious hepatic, renal, or cardiovascular disease, use of medications or supplements that could strongly affect body weight or glucose metabolism, and any other condition judged by the investigators to interfere with participation. Eligible participants were randomly assigned in a 1:1 ratio to receive either a test beverage containing green tea catechins and inulin (catechin + inulin group) or a placebo beverage (placebo group). Randomization was conducted by an independent third party using a stratified allocation procedure with sex, age, baseline VFA, and HbA1c as stratification factors. Strata were defined by sex (male/female) and by dichotomizing age, baseline VFA, and HbA1c at their respective medians. Within each stratum, allocation was performed using a permuted block design (block size = 2). Randomization sequences were generated by a computer program and implemented by an independent person responsible for allocation. Group assignments were concealed from participants, investigators, outcome assessors, and data analysts throughout the study. The intervention period was 12 weeks. Study visits were conducted at baseline (week 0), week 4, week 8, and week 12. Recruitment was conducted from May to June 2023, and follow-up was performed from 1 July to 30 October 2023. No important changes to the trial methods or outcomes were made after trial commencement. Participant enrollment was performed by the operational coordinator, and allocation was conducted by the lead statistician. No patients were enrolled; all participants were community-dwelling Japanese adults residing in Hirosaki City, Aomori Prefecture, Japan. Patients and the public were not involved in the design, conduct, reporting, or dissemination plans of this research.

2.2. Intervention Participants in the catechin + inulin group consumed one bottle per day of a test powdered beverage containing green tea catechins (400 mg/day) and water-soluble dietary fiber in the form of inulin (2.3 g/day), dissolved in 350 mL of water or hot water. Participants in the placebo group consumed a matched powdered beverage without catechins or inulin, dissolved in 350 mL of water or hot water. The powdered beverages were identical in appearance, taste, and pillows-style packaging, and were labeled with anonymized identification stickers. Participants were instructed to consume the powdered beverage, without a specified intake time, for 12 consecutive weeks, and to maintain their usual diet and physical activity during the study. The 12-week intervention period was selected because previous catechin intervention trials assessing visceral fat and metabolic outcomes have commonly used a similar duration. The catechin dose (400 mg/day) was chosen to fall within the range used in prior human studies, whereas the inulin dose (2.3 g/day) was selected as a practical, beverage-compatible amount intended for feasible long-term daily intake. Adherence was monitored by counting returned pillow packs and by self-reported intake records. Participants were asked to avoid initiating new supplements or medications that could influence weight, lipid profile, or glucose metabolism during the trial.

2.3. Outcomes and Measurements The primary outcome was the absolute change in VFA from baseline (week 0) to week 12. VFA was estimated by the abdominal bio-impedance method using EW-FA90 (Panasonic Corporation, Osaka, Japan), an approved medical device in Japan (No. 22500BZX00522000). Measurement values from this device have been reported to have a strong correlation with those obtained using CT, which is the gold standard for measuring visceral fat. Secondary outcomes included body weight, BMI, waist circumference, systolic and diastolic blood pressure (SBP and DBP), fasting plasma glucose (Glu), HbA1c, fasting immunoreactive insulin (IRI), lipid profile (triglycerides [TG], HDL cholesterol [HDL-C], and LDL cholesterol [LDL-C]), liver enzymes (aspartate aminotransferase [AST], alanine aminotransferase [ALT], and gamma-glutamyl transferase [GGT]), liver fat assessed by CAP, and gut microbiota composition at the genus level. Body weight and waist circumference were measured in light clothing without shoes. BMI was calculated as weight (kg) divided by height squared (m 2 ). Blood pressure was measured in the seated position after at least 5 min of rest using an automated sphygmomanometer. Fasting blood samples were drawn in the morning after an overnight fast and analyzed at a certified laboratory using standard methods. HOMA-IR was calculated as: HOMA-IR = [fasting glucose (mg/dL) × fasting insulin (µU/mL)]/405. CAP, an index of liver steatosis, was measured using FibroScan ® (Echosens, Paris, France) with a transient elastography probe according to standard procedures. Examinations were considered unreliable and excluded if fewer than 10 valid measurements were obtained or if measurement quality criteria were not met.

2.4. Gut Microbiota Analysis Stool samples were collected at baseline and week 12 in Metabolokeeper ® (TechnoSuruga Laboratory Co., Ltd., Shizuoka, Japan) [ 14 ]. After collection, samples were homogenized in the buffer and stored at 4 °C until DNA extraction. Microbial community profiling was performed by amplicon sequencing of the V3–V4 region of the prokaryotic 16S rRNA gene. Briefly, DNA was extracted from the stool suspensions, and the V3–V4 region was amplified using universal primers that allow simultaneous detection of Bacteria and Archaea, as developed and validated by Takahashi et al. [ 15 ]. Paired-end sequencing was conducted on an Illumina MiSeq platform (Illumina, San Diego, CA, USA), following a workflow similar to that used in previous Japanese microbiome studies [ 16 ]. Raw demultiplexed paired-end 16S rRNA gene reads were processed in QIIME 2 (version 2024.2). Primer removal, quality filtering, dereplication, paired-end merging, and denoising (including chimera removal) were performed with DADA2 (dada2 denoise-paired). Resulting amplicon sequence variants (ASVs) were taxonomically classified using a Greengenes 13_8 (gg-13-8-99) Naive Bayes classifier. For downstream analyses, ASV counts were collapsed to genus level and converted to relative abundance per sample; relative abundance was calculated as the proportion of reads assigned to each taxon out of the total reads in that sample.

2.5. Sample Size Calculation The required sample size was estimated based on the expected between-group difference in the 12-week change in VFA. A prior pooled analysis of catechin intervention trials in Japanese adults [ 4 ] suggested that catechin intake could reduce VFA by approximately 9.5 cm 2 relative to controls, with a standard deviation of approximately 16.8 cm 2 . Assuming a between-group difference of 9.51 cm 2 , a common SD of 16.81 cm 2 , a two-sided α of 0.05, and 80% power, we calculated that 51 participants per group (102 total) would be required. Allowing for an anticipated dropout rate of 10%, we set a planned enrollment target of 57 participants per group (114 total). However, owing to recruitment feasibility and time constraints, enrollment was stopped at 96 randomized participants. Under the original assumptions, this sample size corresponds to an estimated power of approximately 78%.

3.1. Participant Flow and Baseline Characteristics Of the 96 participants randomized (catechin + inulin group, n = 49; placebo group, n = 47), two participants in the catechin + inulin group and three in the placebo group discontinued the study for personal reasons during the intervention period. VFA measurements at both baseline and week 12 were available for 47 participants in the catechin + inulin group and 44 participants in the placebo group, comprising the FAS for the primary outcome ( Figure 1 ). Baseline characteristics of the FAS are summarized in Table 1 . The two groups were generally well balanced at baseline with respect to age, sex distribution, VFA, body weight, BMI, waist circumference, SBP, DBP, fasting glucose, HbA1c, lipid profile (TG, HDL-C, and LDL-C), and liver enzymes (AST, ALT, and GGT). There were no significant between-group differences in these baseline variables. Adherence to the study beverages was high in both groups, and the number of participants with intake rates below 80% was small. Regarding safety, the overall incidence of adverse events was similar between groups, and most events were mild and resolved spontaneously. No serious adverse events with a plausible relationship to the study beverages were observed, and no participant discontinued the study because of safety concerns. AST and ALT showed small increases in the catechin + inulin group compared with the placebo group; although these differences were statistically significant, all values remained within the normal range, and no clinically meaningful hepatic or renal impairment was detected.

sections.section_title: 3.2. Changes in Visceral Fat Area and Anthropometric Parameters
sections.section_text: 3.2. Changes in Visceral Fat Area and Anthropometric Parameters Changes in VFA and other anthropometric measures from baseline to week 12 are presented in Table 2 . The primary outcome, VFA, increased slightly in both groups over 12 weeks, with a mean change of +0.91 cm 2 in the catechin + inulin group and +4.61 cm 2 in the placebo group. The between-group difference in VFA change was not statistically significant ( p = 0.243), indicating that the intervention did not produce a clear reduction in VFA compared with placebo under the present conditions ( Figure 2 ). Body weight, BMI, and waist circumference showed small decreases in the catechin + inulin group and small increases in the placebo group, but the between-group differences in changes were not statistically significant. Similarly, SBP and DBP did not differ significantly between groups in terms of 12-week changes ( Figure 2 ). In sex-stratified analyses, women showed a numerically greater decrease in VFA, whereas men showed numerically greater decreases in body weight and BMI; however, none of the between-group differences reached statistical significance ( Supplementary Table S1 ).

sections.section_title: 3.3. Changes in Metabolic and Liver-Related Parameters
sections.section_text: 3.3. Changes in Metabolic and Liver-Related Parameters Changes in metabolic parameters and liver-related indices are shown in Table 2 . LDL-C tended to decrease slightly in the catechin + inulin group and increase in the placebo group, but the between-group difference did not reach statistical significance. TG and HDL-C also showed no significant between-group differences in change. AST and ALT increased modestly in the catechin + inulin group, whereas they remained relatively stable in the placebo group, resulting in statistically significant between-group differences. However, these changes were small in magnitude and remained within the normal reference ranges, suggesting limited clinical relevance. GGT and other liver and renal function markers did not show notable or clinically concerning between-group differences. CAP, an index of liver fat, changed by −2.0 ± 58.5 dB/m (n = 44) in the catechin + inulin group and by −6.8 ± 64.1 dB/m (n = 42) in the placebo group over 12 weeks, with no significant between-group difference ( p = 0.72).

3.4. Changes in Insulin Resistance (HOMA-IR) HOMA-IR was evaluated as a key marker of insulin resistance. Baseline HOMA-IR was comparable between groups. From baseline to week 12, HOMA-IR decreased in the catechin + inulin group and increased in the placebo group (mean change: −0.32 ± 1.71 vs. +0.18 ± 0.76), with a statistically significant between-group difference ( p = 0.020). Fasting IRI also tended to decrease in the catechin + inulin group and increase in the placebo group (mean change: −1.05 ± 5.55 vs. +0.73 ± 2.59), ( p = 0.009). In contrast, fasting glucose and HbA1c exhibited minimal changes in both groups, with no significant between-group differences.

Limitations This study has several limitations. First, the sample size was smaller than originally planned, leading to limited statistical power to detect modest differences in VFA and other secondary endpoints. Based on the effect size assumptions from previous pooled analyses [ 4 ], our final sample size provided an estimated power of approximately 78% for the primary endpoint. Because this is slightly below the conventional 80% threshold, we cannot rule out the possibility of a Type II error; if the actual effect size of the intervention was smaller than initially assumed, true differences in VFA might have been undetected. Second, the dose of inulin was substantially lower than in many prior ITF trials [ 8 , 9 ], and the catechin dose was at the lower end of the effective range reported for visceral fat reduction [ 3 , 4 , 5 ]. Third, the intervention period was relatively short (12 weeks); longer-term interventions might be necessary to observe more pronounced changes in visceral fat, liver fat, and gut microbiota composition. Fourth, the gut microbiota analyses were exploratory and involved multiple comparisons, resulting in a lack of significant taxa after FDR correction. Dietary intake was not strictly controlled and was assessed under free-living conditions; thus, residual dietary variation could have influenced the gut microbiota. In addition, the microbiota analyses were exploratory and may have been underpowered to detect subtle but consistent between-group differences. Accordingly, the findings, particularly those for secondary outcomes and exploratory microbiota analyses, should be interpreted with appropriate caution. Despite these limitations, the present trial has several strengths. It employed a double-blind randomized design, targeted adults with objectively measured visceral obesity, and simultaneously evaluated VFA, insulin resistance, liver fat, and gut microbiota. The observed improvement in HOMA-IR, together with the exploratory association between Coprococcus changes and HOMA-IR, suggests that even a relatively low dose of inulin combined with green tea catechins may influence insulin resistance in adults with visceral obesity, possibly via alterations in SCFA-producing gut bacteria.

5. Conclusions In this double-blind randomized controlled trial involving Japanese adults with visceral obesity, a 12-week intake of a beverage containing green tea catechins (400 mg/day) and inulin (2.3 g/day) did not significantly reduce the visceral fat area compared with the placebo. Insulin resistance assessed by HOMA-IR improved; however, this was a secondary endpoint and should be interpreted cautiously. Exploratory analyses suggested an inverse association between changes in Coprococcus and changes in HOMA-IR; however, no taxa remained significant after FDR correction, and these microbiota findings are hypothesis-generating. Overall, these results suggest that this practical dietary intervention may have clinical relevance for insulin resistance and warrant larger, adequately powered trials to confirm the metabolic effects and clarify microbiota-related mechanisms.

1 Introduction Tea, world's second most consumed beverage after water, is valued globally for its distinctive flavor and health-promoting properties. Such attributes are largely determined by the rich and complex profile of secondary metabolites present in tea leaves ( Zhang, Cao, et al., 2020 ). Catechins, a secondary metabolite, stand out as the most characteristic and functionally considerable constituents. Catechins not only contribute to the bitterness and astringency of the tea infusion but also form the chemical basis for its potent antioxidant activity, which in turn mediates various health benefits, such as anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, antiviral, anticancer, and cardiovascular protective effects ( Khaleel et al., 2021 ; Yao et al., 2025 ). Catechins belong to flavan-3-ols, which are characterized by a C6–C3–C6 skeleton comprising two benzene rings (A and B) and a dihydropyran heterocycle (C ring). Catechins are primarily distributed in the tender leaves of tea plants, accounting for 12%–24% of their dry weight and constituting 60%–80% of total polyphenols ( Zeng et al., 2021 ). On the basis of the spatial configuration of the substituents on the C ring, catechins can be classified into cis-catechins (also known as epicatechins) and trans-catechins (nonepicatechins). The former primarily includes epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG), epigallocatechin (EGC), epicatechin gallate (ECG), and epicatechin (EC), while the latter comprises catechin (C), gallocatechin (GC), and gallocatechin gallate (GCG). Previous studies have demonstrated that trans-catechins exhibit superior physiological activities to their cis-isomers owing to their stereochemical configuration. Their spatial arrangement confers a more compact and stable molecular architecture, resulting in enhanced oxidative resistance and sustained bioactive performance ( Shi et al., 2016 ). A relevant example is GCG, which demonstrates stronger antiallergic, antioxidant, and cholesterol-lowering activities than its cis-analog EGCG, although both share an identical molecular formula ( Ikeda et al., 2004 ; Jovanovic et al., 1995 ). Regarding sensory quality, trans-catechins exhibit a more favorable taste profiles than their cis-counterparts. Under identical concentrations, compounds such as GCG impart less bitterness and astringency ( Scharbert & Hofmann, 2005 ; Xu et al., 2018 ). Thermal processing facilitates the isomerization of cis- to trans-catechins (notably EGCG to GCG). Such a transformation is a key pathway for astringency reduction and mellower infusions production ( Lu et al., 2023 ; Xue et al., 2023 ). However, the efficiency of such processing-induced conversion is limited. The identification of natural tea germplasm that is inherently rich in trans-catechins offers a more efficient alternative for quality enhancement. In commonly cultivated tea plants ( Camellia sinensis ), cis-catechins predominate as the primary constituents and EGCG is the most abundant catechin, followed by ECG, EGC, and EC. The four cis-catechin monomers account for >95% of the total catechins. However, trans-catechins, especially GCG, are present at extremely low levels (<1 mg/g or undetectable), overall accounting for only ∼2% of total catechins ( Jin et al., 2014 ; Luo et al., 2025 ). In some wild tea germplasms, such as Camellia ptilophylla (cocoa tea), trans-catechins have been identified as the predominantly accumulated form. Compared with conventional tea cultivars, unfermented green tea or lightly fermented white tea derived from cocoa tea exhibits enhanced antioxidant and free radical–scavenging capacities ( Peng et al., 2011 ). Its water extract effectively inhibits tumor growth; it is a potential functional beverage that can prevent or treat colorectal cancer ( Gao et al., 2020 ). However, owing to reproductive isolation and technical challenges in vegetative propagation, the practical application of such wild tea germplasm remains limited ( Yang, Wei, et al., 2016 ; Zheng et al., 2023 ). Herein, a unique cultivated tea germplasm, Y5 ( C. sinensis var. pabilimba Chang), characterized by exceptionally high trans-catechin contents, was identified. The metabolic profile of Y5 and its associated flavor components is analyzed using metabolomics, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), and liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS/MS). Transcriptomics and gene co-expression network analysis further revealed the molecular regulatory mechanism underlying GCG hyperaccumulation. Ultimately, this study aims to provide superior raw material for enhanced tea beverages and insights into the biosynthetic pathway of trans-catechins.

2 Materials and methods 2.1 Plant materials While selecting and breeding new tea cultivars, a series of promising new strains with desirable traits—designated as Y1, Y2, Y3, Y4, and Y5—were developed via natural hybridization. Subsequently, the strains were propagated through cuttings and planted in the experimental tea garden of the Tea Research Institute, Fujian Academy of Agricultural Sciences, in Fu'an (119°34′39′′E, 27°12′57′′N, Fujian, China). Plants were cultivated under uniform environmental conditions and received identical agronomic management. In April 2022, fresh samples (one bud and two leaves) were collected by the same researcher in the evening (3:00–4:00 PM) from a total of 10–12 individual plants per genotype ( Fig. 1 ). For each of the five genotypes (Y1–Y5), three independent biological replicates were generated, with each replicate comprising pooled tissue from 3 to 4 different plants grown under identical conditions. Parts of materials were vacuum-dried using a vacuum dryer (Scientz-100F, Ningbo, China) and ground into powders using a globe mill (Retsch-MM 400, Haan, Germany) for biochemical component analysis. The remaining samples were subjected to liquid nitrogen grinding for RNA extraction. Fig. 1 Series of new tea strains and samples exhibiting one bud and two leaves. Fig. 1 2.2 Metabolomic and transcriptomic profiling analysis The relative metabolome and transcriptome analysis were accomplished by Metware Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan, China) described by Hao et al. (2023) . Details are as follows: For metabolomic analysis, 50-mg samples were weighed and dissolved in 1.2 mL of 70% (v/v) methanol. The resulting single extract was then processed through six cycles of vortexing: an initial 30-s vortex followed by five additional 30-s vertexing steps at 30-min intervals. The final supernatant was filtered through 0.22-μm filters (Millipore, Bradford, USA). Each sample was performed for three biological replicates and subjected to ultra-performance liquid chromatography (UPLC, SHIMADZU Nexera X2, Tokyo, Japan) and tandem mass spectrometry (MS/MS, Applied Biosystems 4500 QTRAP, SCIEX, Concord, Canada) for metabolite analysis. A chromatographic column C18 (1.8 μm, 2.1 mm × 100 mm, Agilent, Santa Clara, USA) was used. Mobile phase consisted of solvent A (ultrapure water with 0.1% formic acid) and solvent B (acetonitrile with 0.1% formic acid). The reagents formic acid and acetonitrile were purchased from Merck KGaA (Darmstadt, Germany). A gradient elution program was applied as follows: 0–9 min, 5%–95% B; 9–10 min, maintained at 95% B; 10–11.1 min, 95%–5% B; and 11.1–14 min, maintained at 5% B for column re-equilibration. The flow rate was set to 0.35 mL/min, the column temperature was set at 40 °C, and the injection volume was 4 μL. The mass spectrometry data were then collected in positive/negative electrospray ionization (ESI) mode. The ESI source operation parameters were as follows: source temperature 550 °C; ion spray voltage 5500 V (positive ion mode)/−4500 V (negative ion mode); ion source gas I, gas II, curtain gas was set at 345, 414, and 172 kPa, respectively; the collision-activated dissociation was set to high. Instrument tuning and mass calibration were performed with 10 and 100 μmol/L polypropylene glycol solutions in triple quadrupole (QQQ) and linear ion trap (LIT) modes, respectively. QQQ scans were acquired as multiple reaction monitoring (MRM) experiments with collision gas (nitrogen) set to medium. The declustering potential (DP) and collision energy (CE) were optimized for each MRM ion pair. A specific set of MRM transitions were monitored for each period according to the metabolites eluted within this period. To ensure instrument stability and data reliability, 2-chlorophenylalanine (1 ppm; purchased from J&K Scientific, Beijing, China) was used as an internal standard. A pooled quality control (QC) sample was prepared by mixing all of the test sample extracts. Throughout the LC-MS/MS analytical run, QC samples were injected at regular intervals. The total ion chromatograms (TICs) of all QC injections were overlaid. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of all detected metabolites in QC samples was ≤30%, confirming satisfactory instrument stability and data reproducibility. Metabolite identification was performed using the Metware Database (MWDB), a self-built library of authentic standards analyzed under identical LC-MS/MS conditions. Identification was based on matching accurate mass, MS/MS fragmentation patterns, isotopic distribution of MS/MS fragments, and retention time (RT) against the database, with MS and MS/MS tolerances set at 20 ppm and RT tolerance at 0.2 min. Confidence levels were assigned as follows: Level 1 (MS/MS spectrum + RT match score ≥ 0.7), Level 2 (MS/MS + RT match score 0.5–0.7), and Level 3 (Q1, Q3, RT, DP, and CE matched database entries without MS/MS confirmation). Quantification was performed using scheduled multiple reaction monitorin

sections.section_title: Determination of catechin and alkaloid contents
sections.section_text: 2.3 Determination of catechin and alkaloid contents Catechin and alkaloid contents were measured using the HPLC system according to Zhang, Wang, et al. (2020) . A 0.2-g tea sample was extracted using 70% methanol, centrifuged, and filtered through a 0.45-μm filters (Millipore, Bradford, USA) before injection. Component analysis was performed on a HPLC system (Waters Corporation, Milford, USA) equipped with a C12 reverse-phase column (4.6 mm × 250 mm, 5 μm, Phenomenex, Torrance, USA). The mobile phase consisted of 1% formic acid (A), acetonitrile (B), and ultrapure water (D) using the following linear gradient: 0–42 min, 4%–18.7% B; 42–43 min, 18.7%–4% B. The reagents formic acid and acetonitrile were purchased from Merck KGaA (Darmstadt, Germany). The ultrapure water was obtained from Milli-Q (Millipore, Bradford, USA). The flow rate was maintained at 1 mL/min, and the detection was performed at 280 nm. Each sample was performed for three biological replicates. Seven monomeric catechins and two alkaloids were detected in this study. All of the standards were purchased from Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The standard calibration curves are presented in Table S2. The cis-catechin contents were calculated by adding the individual contents of EC, EGC, ECG, and EGCG. The trans-catechin contents were calculated by adding the individual contents of C, GC, and GCG. The alkaloid content was calculated by adding the individual contents of theobromine (TB) and caffeine (CAF).

sections.section_title: Determination of proanthocyanidin and anthocyanin contents
sections.section_text: 2.4 Determination of proanthocyanidin and anthocyanin contents Briefly, 50-mg samples were used and dissolved in a 500-μL extraction solvent (50% methanol in water containing 0.1% hydrochloric acid). Methanol was purchased from Merck KGaA (Darmstadt, Germany). Hydrochloric acid was purchased from Xinyang Chemical Reagent (Changsha, China). All of the standards were purchased from isoReag (Shanghai, China). Following vertexing and ultrasonication each for 5 min, supernatant was collected by centrifuging the sample at 12,000 rpm for 3 min. The extraction procedure was repeated once more. The combined extraction solution was filtered through via 0.22-μm filters (Millipore, Bradford, USA) and subjected to LC-MS/MS analysis. Further measurement was accomplished by Metware Biotechnology Co., Ltd., (Wuhan, China). The analytical conditions were as follows, UPLC: column, WatersACQUITY BEH C18 (1.7 μm, 2.1 mm × 100 mm, Waters Corporation, Milford, USA); solvent system, water (0.1% formic acid): methanol (0.1% formic acid); gradient program, 95:5 V/V at 0 min, 50:50 V/V at 6 min, 5:95 V/V at 12 min, hold for 2 min, 95:5 V/V at 14 min; hold for 2 min; flow rate, 0.35 mL/min; temperature, 40 °C; injection volume, 2 μL. The reagents formic acid and acetonitrile were purchased from Merck KGaA (Darmstadt, Germany). LIT and QQQ scans were acquired on the AB Sciex QTRAP 6500 LC-MS/MS platform (SCIEX, Concord, Canada) System, equipped with an ESI Turbo Ion-Spray interface, operating in positive ion mode and controlled by Analyst 1.6.3 software (Sciex). The ESI source operation parameters were as follows: ion source, ESI+; source temperature 550 °C; ion spray voltage (IS) 5500 V; curtain gas (CUR) was set at 35 psi, respectively. Anthocyanins were analyzed using scheduled MRM. Mass spectrometer parameters including the DP and CE for individual MRM transitions were done with further optimization. A specific set of MRM transitions were monitored for each period according to the metabolites eluted within this period. Quantification of target compounds was performed using a combination of absolute quantification (with authentic standards) and semi-relative quantification (with structural analogs). A series of standard solutions at different concentrations were prepared to generate calibration curves for each compound. Based on the retention time and peak shape information of authentic standards, the detected chromatographic peaks were integrated and corrected across samples. Data acquisitions were performed using Analyst 1.6.3 software (Sciex). Multiquant 3.0.3 software (Sciex) was used to quantify all metabolites. To ensure the reliability of quantification, the following validation parameters were determined for all authentic standards used in this study: recovery rates, limits of detection (LOD), limits of quantification (LOQ), calibration curve equations, and linear ranges. Detailed validation data for each compound are provided in Table S3. Three technical replicates were analyzed for each sample, and the final results are expressed as micrograms per gram of dry weight (μg/g DW).

2.5 Determination of amino acid contents Freeze-dried powder of a 60-mg sample was used and dissolved in a 3-mL extraction solvent (20% ethanol in water containing 0.001-M hydrochloric acid). Ethanol was purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, China). Hydrochloric acid was purchased from Xinyang Chemical Reagent (Changsha, China). Subsequently, the sample was sonicated in an ice-water bath for 60 min and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 5 min. Supernatant was collected and filtered via 0.22-μm filters (Millipore, Bradford, USA) and subjected to HPLC–MS/MS (Agilent Technologies, Inc. Santa Clara, USA) analysis. Each sample was performed for three biological replicates. HPLC analytical conditions were as follows: the chromatographic column used was Information-HILICZ (2.7 μm, 3.0 mm × 100 mm, Bonna-Agela Technologies Inc. Tianjin, China), solvent system comprised 75% acetonitrile (solvent A) and 0.1-mol/L sodium acetate (solvent B). Acetonitrile was purchased from Merck KGaA (Darmstadt, Germany). Sodium acetate was purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, China). The linear elution gradient was set as 0–5 min (10% –50% A), 5–8 min (50% A), and 8–8.1 min (50%–10%A). Subsequently, 1-μL samples were eluted at 0.3 mL·min −1 at a 35 °C column temperature. The reagents were purchased from Merck KGaA (Darmstadt, Germany). All of the standards were purchased from Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The mass spectrometry parameters were set as follows: ionization mode: electrospray ionization positive ion mode, scan type: multiple reaction monitoring, curtain gas: 103 kPa, spray voltage: +4000 V, nebulizer gas pressure: 448 kPa, auxiliary gas pressure: 483 kPa, and nebulizer temperature: 400 °C.

2.6 Weighted gene co-expression network analysis Expression levels of the tested genes in transcriptomic profiling were used for weighted gene co-expression network analysis (WGCNA). To enhance the precision of network construction, gene filtering (via varFilter, threshold = 0.3) yielded a set of 21,282 genes, based on which the WGCNA parameters minModuleSize = 1000 and mergeCutHeight = 0.25 were applied to construct a network focused on large, robust co-expression modules. WGCNA was performed using R version 4.2.2 on the online platform Metware Cloud ( http://cloud.metware.cn ). A characteristic matrix for catechin metabolism was constructed using the abundance data of 15 key flavonoids identified in 2.3 , 2.4 . These metabolites encompass three major classes: catechins (EC, EGC, ECG, EGCG, C, GC, GCG), proanthocyanidins (B1, B2, B3, B4), and anthocyanins (Delphinidin-3-O-(6″-O-galloyl) glucoside, Cyanidin-3-O-galactoside, Delphinidin-3-O-galactoside, Cyanidin-3-O-(6″-O-coumaryl) galactoside). This matrix was subsequently employed in the gene significance–module membership (GS–MM) analysis. The significance of Pearson correlation coefficients was assessed with p -values adjusted for multiple comparisons using the Benjamini-Hochberg FDR method. Gene nodes in the blue module were further filtered by setting a threshold with edge weights >0.3. Subsequently, gene co-expression networks were constructed and visualized on the Cytoscape 3.5.1 software.

sections.section_title: Comparative analysis of metabolomic profiling in five strains
sections.section_text: 3.1 Comparative analysis of metabolomic profiling in five strains To comprehensively understand the changes in the metabolites in new strains of tea plants, broadly targeted metabolomics using UPLC–MS/MS was conducted. Overall, 1147 metabolites that were divided into 11 classifications were detected. Principal component analysis (PCA) showed that the tested samples were clearly divided into five groups; three biological replicates were clustered closely, proving the credibility of metabolomic profiling. Notably Y5 was considerably distinct from other samples, indicating its particularity in metabolomic profiling ( Fig. 2 A). Fig. 2 Metabolomic profiling of the five tea strains. (A) Principal component analysis of metabolomic data. (B) Number of differentially accumulated metabolites identified in comparative analyses. (C) Top 20 co-enriched KEGG pathways using Y5 as the control. Fig. 2 To investigate detailed metabolic differences among the five strains, multiple comparative groups were generated via pairwise comparison. The number of DAMs identified in each sample is shown in Fig. 2 B. The most DAMs were obtained using the Y5 as a control; notably, 172 DAMs were screened, which were noticeably higher than in other comparative groups. Of these 172 DAMs, 32 were co-upregulated and 115 were co-downregulated in Y5. The results reconfirmed the specificity of the metabolic mode in Y5. To further clarify the specific metabolisms in Y5, DAMs obtained from each comparison were subjected to KEGG enrichment analysis. The top 20 enriched pathways of DAMs in Y5 are shown in Fig. 2 C. Results demonstrated that flavonoid biosynthesis (ko00941) was the most considerably co-enriched pathway in all of the comparative groups. The upstream and downstream pathways were markedly co-enriched, including phenylpropanoid biosynthesis (ko00940) and flavone/flavonol biosynthesis (ko00944). In addition, some amino acid pathways were also co-enriched, such as phenylalanine (Phe; ko00360), arginine (Arg) and proline (ko00330).

sections.section_title: Catechin and alkaloid contents in the five strains
sections.section_text: 3.2 Catechin and alkaloid contents in the five strains The composition and contents of catechins were measured via HPLC. The catechins chromatograms of the tested samples are shown in Fig. 3 A. The peak patterns of Y5 were clearly different from those of other samples, especially for the extremely higher peak of GCG. The sum of three trans-catechins (C, GC and GCG) in Y1–Y4 ranged from 0.6 ± 0.0 to 3.01 ± 0.1 mg/g. The contents of C, GC, and GCG in Y5 increased sharply, reaching 21.6 ± 0.1, 13.22 ± 0.0, and 70.1 ± 0.3 mg/g, respectively. Notably, GCG in Y5 was 50–121 fold higher than that in other samples ( Fig. 3 B). The contents of EC and EGC in Y5 were 4.24 ± 0.1 and 5.39 ± 0.4 mg/g, respectively, which were observed to markedly reduce at the level of p < 0.001 ( Fig. 3 C). In addition, the total catechin content was the highest in Y5 and reached statistical significance ( Fig. 3 D). The component and proportion of catechins dramatically changed in Y5 as well. The percentage of trans-catechins in Y5 reached 54.3% ± 0.1%, which was far higher than that in other samples with 3.0% ± 0.1%–4.3% ± 0.0% ( Fig. 3 E). In addition, Y5 exhibited the highest levels of TB, CAF, and total alkaloids ( Fig. 3 F). The CAF content in Y5 was 44.2 ± 0.2 mg/g, which was comparable to Y1. However, the TB content in Y5 was 16.6 ± 0.1 mg/g, considerably higher than that in other samples, which ranged from 1.6 ± 0.0 mg/g to 5.7 ± 0.2 mg/g. Fig. 3 Catechin and alkaloid contents in the five tea strains. (A) HPLC chromatograms of catechins and alkaloids. (B) Trans-catechin contents. (C) Cis-catechin contents. (D) Total catechin contents. (E) Proportion and composition of catechins. (F) Contents and composition of alkaloids. * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, and *** p < 0.001. p -values were obtained using Student's t -test. Fig. 3

sections.section_title: Other related flavonoid contents in the five strains
sections.section_text: 3.3 Other related flavonoid contents in the five strains Proanthocyanidins (PAs), known as condensed tannins, were formed by polymerizing monomeric catechins. The main adjacent subunits in tea plants were B-type PAs, namely B1 (EC-C), B2 (EC-EC), B3 (C—C), and B4 (C-EC) ( Yu et al., 2022 ; Zhuang et al., 2020 ). The components and contents of the four PAs considerably varied across the five strains ( Fig. 4 A). Of the four PAs, B4 showed the highest level, ranging from 1536.5 ± 50.2 to 2054.7 ± 47.7 μg/g. In Y5, the contents of B1 and B3, which use trans-catechin (C) as basic extension units, were markedly increased. By contrast, the contents of B2 and B4, which use cis-catechin (EC) as basic extension units, showed a decreasing trend. Such an accumulation pattern of PAs in Y5 was highly consistent with their trans- and cis-catechin profiles. In addition, no notable changes in the total content of PAs were observed between Y5 and the other strains. However, the composition of PAs in Y5 remarkably altered, with an increased ratio of B1 and B3, and a decreased ratio of B2 and B4 (Fig. S1). Fig. 4 Contents of proanthocyanidins (A) and anthocyanins (B) in the five tea strains. * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, and *** p < 0.001. p -values were obtained using Student's t -test. Fig. 4 Anthocyanins, an essential subclass of flavonoids in tea plants, were quantified and analyzed using targeted metabolomics in this study. Overall, 92 anthocyanins were detected and could be divided into six classes, namely 25 cyanidin, 26 delphinidin, 11 malvidin, 9 pelargonidin, 16 peonidin, and five petunidin (Table S4). From the tested samples with maximum value of ≥5 μg/g, four components were selected; their contents are shown in Fig. 4 B. All four components showed the highest level in Y5. The most abundant component was delphinidin-3-O-(6″-O-galloyl) glucoside, followed by cyanidin-3-O-galactoside, delphinidin-3-O-galactoside, and cyanidin-3-O-(6″-O-coumaryl) galactoside. Notably, the content of delphinidin-3-O-(6″-O-galloyl) glucoside in Y1–Y4 was <0.2 μg/g, while its level in Y5 increased to 118.2 ± 2.6 μg/g. The results indicated that the content and composition of anthocyanidins changed dramatically in Y5 as well.

sections.section_title: Amino acid contents in the five strains
sections.section_text: 3.4 Amino acid contents in the five strains To assess the changes in amino acid metabolism, the contents of 17 components were measured using the HPLC–MS system. The content of total amino acids (TAAs) in the in tested samples ranged from 19.8 ± 0.2 to 22.8 ± 0.2 mg/g. Theanine (Thea) was the dominant and characteristic amino acid component in tea leaves. TAA and Thea showed the lowest level and markedly decreased in Y5 ( Fig. 5 A). Contents of the other amino acid components are shown in Fig. 5 B and C. Of these components, threonine (Thr), alanine (Ala), valine (Val), leucine (Leu), and Phe showed similar accumulation patterns and considerably decreased in Y5. Notably, Arg content in Y5 was markedly increased. Related metabolic pathway, Arg and proline (ko00330), was also co-enriched through KEGG analysis of DAMs ( Fig. 2 C). The results revealed that reduced TAA content in Y5 resulted from the declined levels of Thea, Thr, Ala, Val, Leu, and Phe. Fig. 5 Contents of amino acids in the five tea strains. TAA, total amino acid; Thea, theanine; Glu, glutamate; Thr, threonine; Asp, aspartic acid; Ala, alanine; Ser, serine; Arg, arginine; Pro, proline; His, histidine; Met, methionine; Val, valine; Leu, leucine; Tyr, tyrosine; Phe, phenylalanine; Ile, isoleucine; Lys, lysine; and Gly, glycine. * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, and *** p < 0.001. p -values were obtained using Student's t -test. Fig. 5

sections.section_title: Comparative analysis of transcriptomic profiling in five strains
sections.section_text: 3.5 Comparative analysis of transcriptomic profiling in five strains To further reveal the molecular mechanism, transcriptomic analysis was performed. PCA revealed that the three replicates were closely clustered and can be clearly divided into five groups. Y5 was noticeably separate from other samples. Taking each sample as a control, 20 comparative groups were generated via pairwise comparison. The most DEGs were observed in Y5, with 1811 co-upregulated and 687 co-downregulated DEGs (Fig. S2). The results were consistent with the metabolic profiling analysis, strongly validating the specificity of metabolic and transcriptomic modes in Y5. Moreover, to verify the reliability of transcriptomic profiling, 12 candidate DEGs were selected for qRT-PCR analysis. As shown in Fig. S3, the expression patterns of these DEGs between transcriptome sequencing and qRT-PCR analysis were highly consistent. The results indicated that the transcriptomic data were reliable and could be used for further analysis. To further clarify the functional classification of DEGs in Y5, GO enrichment analysis of DEGs obtained from each comparison was conducted. The top 20 GO terms of gene functions are shown in Fig. 6 A. Results indicated that DEGs annotated as UDP-glucosyltransferase (UGT) activity (GO:0035251) were markedly and consistently enriched in the four comparisons. Subsequently, 23 candidate UGTs exhibiting a maximum FPKM ≥20 were screened in the tested samples; their expression heatmap is shown in Fig. 6 B. These UGTs were divided into three subgroups, and 11 UGTs showed markedly higher expression levels in Y5. Fig. 6 Transcriptomic profiling of the five tea strains. (A) GO enrichment analysis of DEGs using Y5 as the control. (B) Expression levels of UGTs. (C) Expression levels of the genes involved in flavonoid biosynthesis. PAL, phenylalanine ammonia lyase; C4H, cinnamate-4-hydroxylase; 4CL, 4-coumaroyl-CoA ligase; CHS, chalcone synthase; CHI, chalcone isomerase; F3H, flavanone-3-hydroxylase; FLS, flavonol synthase; F3′H, flavonoid 3′- hydroxylase; DFR, dihydroflavonol-4-reductase; ANR, anthocyanidin reductase; LAR, leucoanthocyanidin reductase; and ANS, anthocyanidin synthase. Fig. 6 In addition, 22 DEGs involved in the flavonoid synthesis pathway were selected for heatmap analysis. As shown in Fig. 6 C, the expression patterns of 22 DEGs were complicated. Coordinated rules were not observed in most of these genes, even for the ones belonging to the same gene family. Two FLS (TGY035030 and TGY081517) and one ANR (TGY074368) showed highly consistent expression patterns and were notably down-regulated in Y5. The results suggested the key roles of the abovementioned genes, namely UGTs, FLS, and ANR, in regulating flavonoid metabolism in the special tea germplasm Y5.

sections.section_title: Identification of regulatory networks and hub genes involved in the catechin pathway
sections.section_text: 3.6 Identification of regulatory networks and hub genes involved in the catechin pathway To further investigate the dramatic changes in catechin-related compounds present in Y5, WGCNA was performed to identify hub genes associated with their metabolic regulation. Overall, 21,282 genes were divided into 13 co-expression modules labeled using different colors. The module–trait relationships of the group samples were analyzed, and a blue module comprising 2341 genes was identified as the specific module in Y5 ( Fig. 7 A). These genes showed a significantly positive correlation with those the 15 metabolites, with a correlation coefficient of 0.98 ⁎⁎ ( p < 0.01). GS–MM analysis further strongly confirmed the close connection between the blue module and relative catechin traits. Trans -Catechins, such as C, GC, and GCG, were observed to be significantly positively correlated with the genes in the blue module ( Fig. 7 B–D). Consistent results were observed in two PAs (B1 and B3) and four anthocyanins (Fig. S4). Furthermore, the correlation network in the blue module was analyzed using Cytoscape software. Hub genes were identified, including MATEs and SCPLs. The findings revealed the importance of the role played by the hub genes in dramatically changing catechin composition, as observed in Y5 ( Fig. 7 E). Fig. 7 WGCNA analysis of the genes in the five tea strains. (A) Module–sample correlations. Each row represents one module, and each column represents a specific tea strain. The value in each cell at the row-column intersection indicates the correlation coefficient between the module and the sample. (B) GS–MM analysis of trans-catechin C traits in the blue module. (C) GS–MM analysis of the trans-catechin GC traits in the blue module. (D) GS–MM analysis of the trans-catechin GCG traits in the blue module. (E) SCPLs and MATEs were identified as hub genes in the blue module. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.) Fig. 7

sections.section_title: Y5 was identified as a unique tea germplasm exhibiting distinct catechin composition
sections.section_text: 4.1 Y5 was identified as a unique tea germplasm exhibiting distinct catechin composition Herein, metabolomic profiling revealed a markedly different accumulation profile of metabolites in Y5. Pairwise comparisons with Y5 and KEGG enrichment analysis revealed that DAMs were markedly co-enriched in flavonoid biosynthesis and its upstream pathway, phenylpropanoid biosynthesis ( Fig. 2 ). Further HPLC analysis revealed the hyperaccumulation of trans-catechins in Y5, while cis-catechins, such as EC and EGC, were at markedly lower levels. Y5 showed distinctive accumulation patterns of catechin compositions, with trans-catechins (C, GC, and GCG) accounting for >50% of the total catechins. The content and proportion of GCG even exceeded those of EGCG ( Fig. 3 A–E). Moreover, the composition of polymeric catechins, such as PAs, showed consistent accumulated patterns with those of catechin monomers ( Fig. 4 A and Fig. S1). PAs with higher proportions of cis-catechins, such as B2 and B4, were perceived to be more astringent and bitter ( Ma et al., 2014 ; Yu et al., 2023 ). Thus, the altered profiles of monomeric or polymeric catechins in Y5 contributed to an enhanced taste of tea infusion. Similar catechin compositions were observed in several wild tea plants, such as cocoa tea ( Jin et al., 2018 ; Zhang et al., 2025 ). A typical biochemical composition of cocoa tea could be characterized by high TB content (20–60 mg/g) and extremely low CAF content (<5 mg/g, or even undetectable) ( Chen et al., 2024 ). However, Y5 contains abundant TB (16.6 ± 0.1 mg/g) and CAF (44.2 ± 0.2 mg/g) ( Fig. 3 F). Therefore, Y5 was identified as a unique tea germplasm exhibiting dramatically changed catechins and alkaloids, distinct from cultivated tea varieties ( C. sinensis ) and wild tea germplasms ( C. ptilophylla Chang). Whether this coordinated accumulation results from direct pathway crosstalk, shared regulatory factors, or independent metabolic shifts remains to be determined. Elucidating the mechanisms linking catechin and alkaloid metabolism in Y5 warrants further investigation. Y5 was a natural hybrid offspring of Lechangbaimaocha ( C. sinensis var. pabilimba Chang); it showed pronounced advantages in plant morphology and was extensively cultivated through cutting propagation by our institute (Tea Research Institute, Fujian Academy of Agricultural Sciences). Based on its improved flavor metabolic profile, which was characterized by hyperaccumulation of trans-catechins (especially GCG), Y5 is suitable for producing white or green tea with lower bitterness and enhanced bioactivity. This tea beverages hold promise for the prevention or treatment of colorectal cancer ( Peng et al., 2011 ; Xue et al., 2023 ). It can also be further processed into tea powders or purified extracts for use as functional ingredients in baked goods and dietary supplements ( Luo et al., 2024 ). Taken together, the novel germplasm Y5 represents a valuable resource for the development and utilization of trans-catechins in functional tea products. Further research is needed to address key aspects of its practical application, including processing adaptability, metabolic stability, sensory properties, and bioactivity validation.

sections.section_title: Profiles of anthocyanins and amino acids chang in Y5
sections.section_text: 4.2 Profiles of anthocyanins and amino acids chang in Y5 Anthocyanins and catechins were derived from the flavonoid pathway and shared common precursors ( Chen et al., 2023 ). Thus, the dramatic changes observed in catechin compositions were expected to markedly impact anthocyanin profiles. This was corroborated by our data, which showed a sharp increase in the accumulations of glycosylated anthocyanins in Y5 ( Fig. 4 B and Table S4). Further analysis of transcriptomic profiling helped identifying key regulatory genes (UGTs, FLS and ANR) at crucial branch points of flavonoid biosynthesis ( Fig. 6 ). The upregulation of UGTs, which catalyzed the glycosylation of anthocyanins for vacuolar sequestration ( Kumari et al., 2020 ), provided a direct mechanistic basis for the increased levels of glycosylated anthocyanins. Concomitantly, the coordinated downregulation of the two competing enzymes was observed: FLS, which channeled metabolic flux toward flavonol synthesis ( Zhao et al., 2025 ) and ANR, which redirected shared anthocyanidin precursors into the cis-catechin branch ( Xu et al., 2024 ). Such a coordinated transcriptional shift attenuated both the alternative pathways, thereby enhancing anthocyanin accumulation and reducing flavonol and cis-catechin biosynthesis in Y5. The amino acid profile of Y5 displayed a decline in the abundance of several components ( Fig. 5 ). This metabolic shift could be attributed to the similar metabolic crosstalk between amino acid and catechins biosynthesis, which was well documented in tea plants ( Tai et al., 2018 ; Zhao et al., 2020 ). Phe was a crucial metabolic node that connected the two pathways ( Aksenova et al., 2024 ). Increased accumulation of total catechins coincided with decreased Phe levels in Y5 ( Fig. 3 and Fig. 5 C). The inverse correlation suggested that enhanced catechin biosynthesis competed for the Phe pool, leading to the observed reduction in amino acids.

sections.section_title: MATEs and SCPLs as crucial contributors to the hyperaccumulation of trans-catechins in Y5
sections.section_text: 4.3 MATEs and SCPLs as crucial contributors to the hyperaccumulation of trans-catechins in Y5 At present, studies on cultivated tea plants focus on cis-catechins EGCG and its biosynthesis is extensively elucidated, while the synthesis of its trans-isomer (GCG) remains poorly understood. MATEs and SCPLs were identified as the hub genes and were observed to be closely linked with the hyperaccumulation of trans-catechins in Y5 through WGCNA ( Fig. 7 ). Recently, the key enzymes responsible for galloylated cis-catechins were identified, with CsSCPL4 acting as a catalytic acyltransferase and CsSCPL5 as a noncatalytic companion paralog. The co-expression of CsSCPL4 and CsSCPL5 necessarily led to the formation of ECG and EGCG when using EC and EGC as substrates, respectively. ( Chen et al., 2025 ; Yao et al., 2022 ). Herein, the two genes that were homologous with CsSCPL4 and CsSCPL5 were identified; they exhibited strong positive correlation with the hyperaccumulation of the related derivatives of trans-catechins ( Fig. 7 ). The correlation suggested that CsSCPL4 and CsSCPL5 could take C and GC as substrates to participate in the biosynthesis of GCG in Y5. Beyond synthesis, the accumulation of secondary metabolites was governed by transport processes, which played a key role in their compartmentalization and storage ( Zhao et al., 2020 ). CsMATE1 and CsMATE2 in Y5 showed sequence homology with CsTT12 ( transparent testa 12), which functioned as cis-catechin transporter in tea plants ( Wang et al., 2024 ). Numerous studies have demonstrated that TT12 homologous proteins in other plants exhibited similar transport activities ( Chen et al., 2018 ; Debeaujon et al., 2001 ; Gao et al., 2016 ; Pal et al., 2023 ). In addition, TT12 orthologs were functionally diversified across species. In Medicago truncatula , MtMATE1 exhibited higher substrate affinity for cis-catechin derivatives, while MtMATE2 preferentially bound with and transported anthocyanins ( Zhao & Dixon, 2009 ; Zhao et al., 2011 ). In Diospyros kaki , DkMATE1 functioned as a cis-catechin derivative transporter and was involved in PA accumulation ( Yang, Jiang, et al., 2016 ), while DkMATE7 exhibited dual functions in transporting cis- and trans-catechins and displayed substrate preferences ( Wu, 2021 ). The findings suggested that the substrate affinity of CsMATEs in Y5 extended to trans-catechins and related derivatives, highlighting the functional role of such transporters in facilitating GCG accumulation. The functions of the key candidate genes CsSCPLs and CsMATEs require further experimental validation through heterologous overexpression, gene silencing, and enzyme activity assays. Moreover, whether CsSCPLs and CsMATEs physically interact to coordinate GCG accumulation remains to be determined. Protein-protein interaction assays (e.g., yeast two-hybrid, co-immunoprecipitation, bimolecular fluorescence complementation) would help elucidate their potential cooperation in trans-catechin biosynthesis and sequestration.

5 Conclusion In summary, a novel germplasm Y5, classified as cultivated tea plants ( C. sinensis var. pabilimba Chang), exhibiting distinctive chemical compositions, was identified. Y5 preferentially accumulated ultra-high levels of trans-catechins (C, GC, and GCG) rather than cis-catechins. Moreover, Y5 contained abundant TB and CAF. The metabolic profiles of other flavor compounds were considerably altered, namely PAs, anthocyanins, and amino acids. The molecular regulatory mechanism of catechins and their derivatives in Y5 is shown in Fig. 8 , which illustrates that the hyperaccumulation of trans-catechins is predominantly governed by downstream genes involved in their synthesis and transport. SCPLs and MATEs could jointly facilitate the accumulation of trans-catechins through coordinated regulation of their biosynthesis and transport. Taken together, a novel phytochemical foundation is established in this work through the identification of a unique tea germplasm Y5, which serves as both a functional model for studying catechin metabolism and a direct source for creating natural tea beverages with enhanced GCG content. Fig. 8 Proposed regulatory mechanism of catechins and their derivatives in Y5. Fig. 8

Appendix A Supplementary data Supplementary material 1 Fig. S1 The contents (A) and composition (B) of PAs in the five tea strains. Fig. S2 Transcriptomic profiling analysis of the five tea strains. Data are mean ± SD from three biological replicates. Fig. S4 GS – MM analysis in blue module using the following components as traits, PA B1 (A), PA B3 (B), delphinidin-3-O-(6″-O-galloyl) glucoside (C), delphinidin-3-O-galactoside (D), cyanidin-3-O-galactoside (E), cyanidin-3-O-(6″-O-coumaryl) galactoside (F). Materials and methods All procedures were conducted in accordance with guidelines approved by the Animal Management Committee of Zhongkai University of Agriculture and Engineering (Approval No. 20220801) and complied with relevant local laws, regulations, and institutional requirements. Preparation of tea polyphenols The tea polyphenol preparation used in this study was produced in the laboratory of the College of Animal Science and Technology, Zhongkai University of Agriculture and Engineering, and contained 50.4% catechins. Animals and experimental design The feeding trial was conducted from August to December 2022 at the Lion-head Goose Science and Technology Backyard in Raoping County, Guangdong Province, China. A completely randomized design with a single factor was used. A total of 240 healthy one-day-old male lion-head geese with similar initial body weight were randomly allocated to 2 treatments (6 replicates/treatment; 20 birds/replicate). The control group received a basal diet, and the tea polyphenol group received the basal diet supplemented with 1,000 mg/kg tea polyphenols. Feed and water were provided ad libitum. Ambient temperature and humidity, and health status (including diarrhea) were recorded daily, and the house was disinfected periodically. The experimental period lasted 18 wk. The basal diet was formulated according to NRC (1994) nutrient requirements for geese and local production practices for lion-head geese in the Chaoshan region. Detailed diet composition and nutrient levels were reported previously ( Fu et al., 2025 ). Sample collection At the end of the trial, birds were fasted for 12 h. Two geese with similar body weight were randomly selected from each replicate. Wing vein blood (10 mL) was collected, allowed to clot, and centrifuged at 3,000 r/min for 15 min to obtain serum. Serum was aliquoted and stored at −80°C for measurement of serum LPS and diamine oxidase (DAO). After slaughter, jejunal and ileal tissues were collected. A portion was fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde for histological analysis, and the remaining tissue was stored at −80°C for determination of antioxidant indices and mRNA expression of antioxidant- and tight-junction–related genes. Cecal contents were collected and stored at −80°C for metagenomic sequencing. Intestinal morphology measurement Paraffin sections were prepared and stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). Villus height and crypt depth in the jejunum and ileum were measured using Image Scope software, and V/C (villus height/crypt depth) was calculated. Serum LPS and DAO assays Serum LPS and DAO were measured using commercial ELISA kits (Jiangsu Enzyme Immunoassay Co., China) according to the manufacturer’s instructions. Determination of intestinal antioxidant indices Antioxidant indices in jejunal and ileal tissues were determined using commercial kits (Jiangsu Enzyme Immunoassay Co., China). Total antioxidant capacity (T-AOC) was measured by a biochemical method, whereas GSH-Px activity, T-SOD activity, and MDA content were determined using ELISA-based assays. Quantitative real-time PCR Total RNA was extracted from jejunal and ileal mucosa using TRIzol. RNA concentration and purity were assessed, and high-quality RNA was reverse-transcribed into cDNA (TaKaRa) and stored at −20°C. Primers for tight-junction– and antioxidant-related genes were designed based on NCBI sequences and synthesized by Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China) ( Table 1 ). Quantitative PCR was performed using PowerUp™ SYBR™ Green Master Mix with 3 technical replicates per sample. β-actin was used as the reference gene, and relative expression was calculated using the 2 ^ - ΔΔCt method. Preparation of tea polyphenols The tea polyphenol preparation used in this study was produced in the laboratory of the College of Animal Science and Technology, Zhongkai University of Agriculture and Engineering, and contained 50.4% catechins.

Animals and experimental design The feeding trial was conducted from August to December 2022 at the Lion-head Goose Science and Technology Backyard in Raoping County, Guangdong Province, China. A completely randomized design with a single factor was used. A total of 240 healthy one-day-old male lion-head geese with similar initial body weight were randomly allocated to 2 treatments (6 replicates/treatment; 20 birds/replicate). The control group received a basal diet, and the tea polyphenol group received the basal diet supplemented with 1,000 mg/kg tea polyphenols. Feed and water were provided ad libitum. Ambient temperature and humidity, and health status (including diarrhea) were recorded daily, and the house was disinfected periodically. The experimental period lasted 18 wk. The basal diet was formulated according to NRC (1994) nutrient requirements for geese and local production practices for lion-head geese in the Chaoshan region. Detailed diet composition and nutrient levels were reported previously ( Fu et al., 2025 ).

Quantitative real-time PCR Total RNA was extracted from jejunal and ileal mucosa using TRIzol. RNA concentration and purity were assessed, and high-quality RNA was reverse-transcribed into cDNA (TaKaRa) and stored at −20°C. Primers for tight-junction– and antioxidant-related genes were designed based on NCBI sequences and synthesized by Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China) ( Table 1 ). Quantitative PCR was performed using PowerUp™ SYBR™ Green Master Mix with 3 technical replicates per sample. β-actin was used as the reference gene, and relative expression was calculated using the 2 ^ - ΔΔCt method. sections.section_title: DNA extraction, library construction, and sequencing
sections.section_text: DNA extraction, library construction, and sequencing Approximately 200–300 mg of cecal content was used for total DNA extraction according to the kit instructions. DNA purity and integrity were evaluated by agarose gel electrophoresis. Qualified DNA was fragmented to ∼350 bp using Covaris ultrasonication and then subjected to end repair, A-tailing, adaptor ligation, purification, and PCR amplification for library construction. Libraries were quantified using Qubit 2.0, assessed for insert size using an Agilent 2100 system, and quantified by qPCR (effective concentration > 3 nM) before sequencing. Sequencing was performed on an Illumina NovaSeq 6000 platform.

sections.section_title: Effects of tea polyphenols on intestinal morphological development
sections.section_text: Effects of tea polyphenols on intestinal morphological development To evaluate mucosal morphological development, histological measurements were performed for the jejunum and ileum. As shown in Fig. 1 , compared with the control, the tea polyphenol group exhibited significantly increased villus height and V/C in both the jejunum and ileum ( P < 0.05), whereas crypt depth did not differ between groups in either intestinal segment ( P > 0.05). Fig. 1 Effects of tea polyphenols on intestinal morphological development in lion-head geese. (A) Measurement of jejunal villus height and crypt depth (scale bar = 600 μm); a and b represent the control and tea polyphenol groups in the jejunum, respectively; c and d represent the control and tea polyphenol groups in the ileum, respectively. (B–D) Villus height, crypt depth, and V/C in the jejunum. (E–G) Villus height, crypt depth, and V/C in the ileum. Asterisks indicate significant differences between groups: * P < 0.05; ** P < 0.01; *** P < 0.001. Fig 1 dummy alt text

sections.section_title: Effects of tea polyphenols on Serum LPS and DAO
sections.section_text: Effects of tea polyphenols on Serum LPS and DAO Serum LPS level and DAO activity reflect intestinal barrier integrity and permeability. As shown in Table 2 , compared with the control, the tea polyphenol group had a significantly lower serum LPS level ( P < 0.05), whereas serum DAO activity did not differ between groups ( P > 0.05). Table 2 Effect of Tea polyphenols on the serum LPS and DAO of lion-head geese. sections.section_title: Effects of tea polyphenols on mRNA expression of tight-junction–related genes
sections.section_text: Effects of tea polyphenols on mRNA expression of tight-junction–related genes The mRNA expression of tight-junction–related genes in the jejunum and ileum is presented in Table 3 . Compared with the control, the tea polyphenol group showed significant upregulation of ZO-1, Claudin-5, and Occludin in the jejunum ( P < 0.05), whereas E-cadherin did not differ ( P > 0.05). In the ileum, ZO-1, Claudin-5, Occludin, and E-cadherin were all significantly upregulated in the tea polyphenol group ( P < 0.05). Table 3 Effects of tea polyphenols on the mRNA expression of genes related to tight-junction proteins in the jejunum and ileum of lion-head geese. sections.section_title: Effects of tea polyphenols on intestinal antioxidant capacity
sections.section_text: Effects of tea polyphenols on intestinal antioxidant capacity Intestinal antioxidant status reflects the mucosal defensive capacity against oxidative stress. As shown in Table 4 , compared with the control, the tea polyphenol group exhibited significantly higher jejunal T-AOC ( P < 0.05), whereas jejunal GSH-Px, T-SOD, and MDA did not differ ( P > 0.05). In the ileum, the tea polyphenol group showed significantly higher GSH-Px and T-SOD activities ( P < 0.05) and significantly lower MDA content ( P < 0.05), whereas ileal T-AOC did not differ between groups ( P > 0.05). Table 4 Effects of tea polyphenols on antioxidant capacity in the jejunum and ileum of lion-head geese. sections.section_title: Effects of tea polyphenols on mRNA expression of antioxidant-related genes
sections.section_text: Effects of tea polyphenols on mRNA expression of antioxidant-related genes The mRNA expression of antioxidant-related genes in the jejunum and ileum is shown in Table 5 . Compared with the control, the tea polyphenol group exhibited significantly higher jejunal mRNA expression of GPX1, GPX2, HO-1, and Nrf2 ( P < 0.05), whereas jejunal SOD1 did not differ ( P > 0.05). In the ileum, the tea polyphenol group showed significant upregulation of SOD1, GPX1, GPX2, HO-1, and Nrf2 ( P < 0.05). Cecal microbial composition and diversity Subsequently, to further evaluate the effects of tea polyphenols on cecal microbial composition and function, metagenomic sequencing was performed on cecal contents. Principal coordinate analysis (PCoA) at the phylum level ( Fig. 2 A) indicated that the microbial communities of the 2 groups were largely separated. Based on NR-based taxonomic annotation ( Fig. 2 B), 12 phyla, 27 classes, 50 orders, 82 families, 143 genera, and 239 species were identified in the cecal bacterial community. Alpha-diversity indices (Chao1, observed_species, and Shannon) did not differ between groups ( P > 0.05) ( Figs. 2 C– 2 E). ANOSIM based on Bray–Curtis distance ( Figs. 2 F– 2 G) further confirmed that between-group differences were greater than within-group differences at both the phylum level ( R = 0.478, P = 0.004) and genus level ( R = 0.826, P = 0.002); moreover, inter-individual variation in the tea polyphenol group appeared relatively larger. Fig. 2 Analysis of cecal microbial composition and diversity. (A) PCoA at the phylum level in the control and tea polyphenol groups. (B) Statistics of annotated taxa. (C) Chao1 index. (D) observed species index. (E) Shannon index. (F) ANOSIM at the phylum level. (G) ANOSIM at the genus level. Fig 2 dummy alt text

sections.section_title: Microbial community structure at the phylum and genus levels
sections.section_text: Microbial community structure at the phylum and genus levels At the phylum level, Fig. 3 A presents the top 10 phyla by relative abundance. Compared with the control, the tea polyphenol group showed significantly higher relative abundance of Actinobacteria ( P < 0.05) and significantly lower relative abundances of Firmicutes and Verrucomicrobia ( P < 0.05) ( Fig. 3 B). At the genus level ( Fig. 3 C), the enrichment pattern of the top 20 genera is shown. Compared with the control, the tea polyphenol group exhibited significantly higher relative abundances of Prevotella, Faecalibacterium, Subdoligranulum, and Olsenella ( P < 0.05), whereas Bacteroides, Oscillibacter, and Flavonifractor were significantly decreased ( P < 0.05) ( Fig. 3 D). Fig. 3 Microbial community structure at the phylum and genus levels. (A–B) Relative abundance of the top 10 phyla and boxplots for significantly different phyla. (C–D) Heatmap of the top 20 genera and boxplots for significantly different genera. Fig 3 dummy alt text

sections.section_title: LEfSe analysis of intestinal microbial communities
sections.section_text: LEfSe analysis of intestinal microbial communities LEfSe analysis was performed to identify the bacterial taxa significantly affected by tea polyphenol supplementation. The cladogram ( Fig. 4 A) showed that differential taxa in the tea polyphenol group were mainly enriched in Proteobacteria and Actinobacteria, whereas those in the control group were mainly enriched in Firmicutes. The LDA histogram (LDA > 2.0, P < 0.05) ( Fig. 4 B) indicated that the tea polyphenol group was enriched in taxa including Collinsella, Coriobacteriaceae, Chlamydiaceae, Chlamydiales, Chlamydia, Chlamydiia, Atopobiaceae, Coriobacteriales, Coriobacteriia, Proteobacteria, and Prevotellaceae, whereas the control group was enriched in taxa including Oscillospiraceae, Verrucomicrobiae, Verrucomicrobiales, Akkermansiaceae, Deltaproteobacteria, Desulfovibrionales, and Desulfovibrionaceae. Fig. 4 LEfSe analysis of differential taxa between control and tea polyphenol groups. (A) Cladogram. (B) LDA bar plot (green and red denote control and tea polyphenol groups, respectively). Fig 4 dummy alt text

sections.section_title: Functional annotation and differential functional analysis
sections.section_text: Functional annotation and differential functional analysis To further characterize microbial functions, functional annotation was conducted using KEGG, eggNOG, and CAZy databases. At KEGG level 1 ( Fig. 5 A), “Metabolism” showed the highest gene abundance, followed by “Genetic Information Processing”. At KEGG level 2 ( Fig. 5 B), the top 3 functional categories in both groups were carbohydrate metabolism, amino acid metabolism, and replication and repair. eggNOG functional annotation counts ( Fig. 5 C) showed that the top 3 categories by gene number were amino acid transport and metabolism (56,561), carbohydrate transport and metabolism (50,357), and replication, recombination and repair (49,007). eggNOG level 3 analysis ( Fig. 5 D) indicated higher relative abundance of replication/recombination/repair and carbohydrate transport and metabolism in the tea polyphenol group than in the control. CAZy annotation ( Fig. 5 E) identified 6 genes assigned to auxiliary activities (AA), 3,267 genes to carbohydrate-binding modules (CBM), 17,183 genes to glycoside hydrolases (GH), 7,583 genes to glycosyltransferases (GT), and 392 genes to polysaccharide lyases (PL). At CAZy level 1 ( Fig. 5 F), the tea polyphenol group exhibited higher relative abundances of GT and PL compared with the control. Fig. 5 Functional annotation and differential functional profiles of the cecal microbiota. (A) KEGG level 1 Circos plot. (B) KEGG level 2 functional relative abundance. (C) eggNOG functional gene counts. (D) eggNOG level 3 relative abundance. (E) CAZy functional gene counts. (F) CAZy level 1 relative abundance. Fig 5 dummy alt text Based on differential analysis of KEGG functional annotation, at level 3 ( Fig. 6 A; Table S2 ), the phosphotransferase system was significantly higher in the tea polyphenol group ( P < 0.05). Differential analysis based on CAZy annotation ( Fig. 6 B; Table S3 ) showed that, at level 2, AA4, CBM13, CBM2, CBM32, GH1, GH25, GH26, GH27, GT1, GT2, GT23, GT25, GT45, and PL1 were significantly higher in the tea polyphenol group than in the control ( P < 0.05). Differential analysis based on eggNOG annotation ( Fig. 6 C; Table S4 ) indicated that, at level 3, Cell cycle control/cell division/chromosome partitioning, Cell wall/membrane/envelope biogenesis, Posttranslational modification/protein turnover/chaperones, and Replication/recombination/repair were significantly higher in the tea polyphenol group ( P < 0.05). To link differential genera with functional shifts, Spearman correlation analyses were performed between representative genera and significantly different KEGG level-3 pathways, CAZy level-2 enzyme families, and eggNOG categories ( Fig. 6 D–F; P < 0.05). In KEGG ( Fig. 6 D; Table S5 ), Prevotella was positively correlated with Thermogenesis and the two - component system but negatively correlated with biotin metabolism and lipoic acid metabolism, whereas Desulfovibrio showed the opposite pattern (positive correlations with biotin metabolism/peroxisome and a negative correlation with Thermogenesis). In CAZy ( Fig. 6 E; Table S6 ), Faecalibacterium was positively correlated with GH3 and PL1. In eggNOG ( Fig. 6 F; Table S7 ), Prevotella was positively correlated with cell wall/membrane/envelope biogenesis. Fig. 6 Heatmaps of functional analysis of the cecal microbiota. (A) Heatmap comparing KEGG level-3 functional pathways between groups. (B) Heatmap comparing CAZy level-2 functional categories between groups. (C) Heatmap comparing eggNOG level-3 functional categories between groups. (D) Spearman correlations between representative genera and the differential KEGG level-3 pathways. (E) Spearman correlations between representative genera and the differential CAZy level-2 enzyme families. (F) Spearman correlations between representative genera and the differential eggNOG level-3 functional categories. Fig 6 dummy alt text

Conclusions Dietary tea polyphenols improved intestinal morphology, barrier-related indices (lower serum LPS and upregulated tight-junction–related gene expression), and antioxidant capacity in lion-head geese. In this study, metagenomic sequencing was employed, revealing changes in the structure and functional potential of the cecal microbiota (e.g., increased Prevotella and Subdoligranulum and decreased Desulfovibrio). Correlation analysis further suggested associations between these differential genera and selected functional metabolic pathways. Overall, these findings support the rational use of tea polyphenols in gut-health management strategies for lion-head goose production.
